EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line, moving 50 to 60 mph, plowed through northeast Kentucky during the late night hours.  This line was well ahead of a strong cold front.  Even though the line of thunderstorms had weakened from the evening hours, wind damage was still common.  Downed trees were widespread.  This event could be labeled as a progressive derecho.  Behind this initial line, a few thunderstorms formed toward dawn and became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down.

Moist northwest flow behind a departing cold front led to a widespread light snow event across eastern Kentucky. Most areas received between 1 and 2 inches of the white stuff from Thursday night November 20th into the early morning hours of Friday November 21st. A few isolated amounts of 3 to 5 inches of snow were reported in the higher terrain of Harlan and Letcher counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained weather observer reported a snowfall of 0.6 inches in Harlan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex storm system brought one and one half to three inches of rain to portions of Eastern Kentucky on December 11th. This led to flash flooding across portions of Letcher County in the evening hours. The flash flooding transitioned to areal flooding on December 12th. Also, flooding due to the Cumberland River at Barbourville running high occurred in Knox County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State police reported that the Gordon Fire Dept had at least 6 inches of running water over the road at the intersection of Highways 17786 and 160.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain was reported in Booneville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tightening pressure gradient ahead of an approaching cold front led to strong, gusty winds across Eastern Kentucky on December 9th. Many wind gusts between 45 and 50 mph were reported. These winds brought down a few trees in McCreary County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds downed numerous trees and power lines in and around Whitley City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tightening pressure gradient ahead of an approaching cold front led to strong, gusty winds across Eastern Kentucky on December 9th. Many wind gusts between 45 and 50 mph were reported. These winds brought down a few trees in McCreary County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees at least 6 inches in diameter were broken off by strong winds 20 to 30 feet above the ground. Wrist sized branches were also broken off trees by the wind. Numerous power outages were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds developed ahead of an approaching cold front on December 24th. Winds gusted between 40 and 50 mph at times. These winds were enough to bring down a few trees and tree limbs across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large limbs were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain fell across portions of Eastern Kentucky late in the afternoon into the evening hours of December 23rd. Light precipitation started out as sleet, freezing rain, and rain during the afternoon hours. By late afternoon, the precipitation had increased in coverage and intensity, and changed over to freezing rain and rain. Arctic air that had been over the area over the previous couple of days allowed for surface temperatures to remain at or below freezing, even as air temperatures rose to near or slightly above freezing. This created very hazardous travel conditions across the area, with numerous accidents reported. Traffic was backed up on the Mountain Parkway due to accidents caused by icy roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain caused icy roads and several car accidents around the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system brought heavy, wet snow to portions of Eastern Kentucky on the evening of December 11th. The snow occurred as colder air moved in behind the departing storm system. Earlier in the day, rain, and some freezing rain, fell across the area. A quick change to snow occurred in the evening. The heavy wet snow stuck to trees, weighing them down and causing many to snap.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At least four inches of snow fell in northern Owsley County near the Lee County border.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex storm system brought a wintry mix of precipitation fell across Eastern Kentucky on December 15th into the 16th. The heaviest precipitation fell mainly along and north of the Mountain Parkway, where freezing rain, sleet, and snow created hazardous travel conditions. A warm front moved north across the area late on the 16th, changing the precipitation to all rain from south to north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to a half inch of sleet and freezing rain created major travel problems through Bath County.  Interstate 64 was closed for several hours due to accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moving across the area brought severe thunderstorms with damaging winds and large hail to portions of Eastern Kentucky during the morning hours of May 11th. Numerous reports of downed trees and power lines were received. Hail up to the size of golf balls was also reported. Two tornadoes touched down as well, with one in Leslie County, and the other in Whitley County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts downed trees and caused damage to roofs and chimneys on homes in the Blair Branch community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mostly clear skies, light winds, and strong high pressure allowed temperatures across the east Kentucky coalfields to drop to or below freezing at most locations during the overnight hours of October 28 and predawn and early morning hours of October 29th, effectively ending JKL's frost/freeze program for the year.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Subfreezing morning temperatures were reported by a trained weather observer.

The heaviest precipitation fell mainly along and north of the Mountain Parkway, where freezing rain, sleet, and snow created hazardous travel conditions. A warm front moved north across the area late on the 16th, changing the precipitation to all rain from south to north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sherriff's Department reported up to three quarters of an inch of sleet and freezing rain accumulation in Frenchburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mostly clear skies, light winds, and strong high pressure allowed temperatures across the east Kentucky coalfields to drop to or below freezing at most locations during the overnight hours of October 28 and predawn and early morning hours of October 29th, effectively ending JKL's frost/freeze program for the year.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Subfreezing morning temperatures were reported by a trained weather observer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex storm system brought one and one half to three inches of rain to portions of Eastern Kentucky on December 11th. This led to flash flooding across portions of Letcher County in the evening hours. The flash flooding transitioned to areal flooding on December 12th. Also, flooding due to the Cumberland River at Barbourville running high occurred in Knox County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Portions of Highway 7 South near Blackey was under 6 to 8 inches of standing water. Water levels have started to recede. There was also some water covering low water crossings in Fleming-Neon along Highway 317.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system brought heavy, wet snow to portions of Eastern Kentucky on the evening of December 11th. The snow occurred as colder air moved in behind the departing storm system. Earlier in the day, rain, and some freezing rain, fell across the area. A quick change to snow occurred in the evening. The heavy wet snow stuck to trees, weighing them down and causing many to snap.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four inches of snow was measured in Annville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moist northwest flow behind a departing cold front led to a widespread light snow event across eastern Kentucky. Most areas received between 1 and 2 inches of the white stuff from Thursday night November 20th into the early morning hours of Friday November 21st. A few isolated amounts of 3 to 5 inches of snow were reported in the higher terrain of Harlan and Letcher counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS employee reported that one inch of snow had fallen in Jackson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving squall line raced through northeast Kentucky as about 50 to 55 mph.  The narrow line of showers, with a few embedded thunderstorms, was just ahead of a strong cold front.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moving across the area brought severe thunderstorms with damaging winds and large hail to portions of Eastern Kentucky during the morning hours of May 11th. Numerous reports of downed trees and power lines were received. Hail up to the size of golf balls was also reported. Two tornadoes touched down as well, with one in Leslie County, and the other in Whitley County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and large limbs were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moving across the area brought severe thunderstorms with damaging winds and large hail to portions of Eastern Kentucky during the morning hours of May 11th. Numerous reports of downed trees and power lines were received. Hail up to the size of golf balls was also reported. Two tornadoes touched down as well, with one in Leslie County, and the other in Whitley County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front was sinking south across the Ohio Valley region during the late afternoon of May 31st. A few thunderstorms developed ahead of the front across Southeastern Kentucky. A couple of the storms were severe, with damaging winds and large hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening warm front over far southern Illinois and western Kentucky became the focus for strong to locally severe thunderstorms. As this warm front moved slowly north, dew points in the upper 50's spread north from Tennessee. Instability was weak, but low-level wind shear was very favorable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The new year started off as an active one, with numerous systems traversing the region. These systems brought rain, snow, and thunder. The frequency of systems caused enough rainfall to push the Green River above flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of the Green River occurred. At the Paradise gage, where flood stage is 380 feet, the river crested at 381.72 feet on the 12th. Minor flooding of bottomland fields occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure over south central Missouri produced an unseasonably mild south wind flow. Thunderstorms with heavy rain developed in the southerly flow ahead of a cold front approaching the Mississippi Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The north folk of Elk Creek was out of its banks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening warm front over far southern Illinois and western Kentucky became the focus for strong to locally severe thunderstorms. As this warm front moved slowly north, dew points in the upper 50's spread north from Tennessee. Instability was weak, but low-level wind shear was very favorable.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Streets were flooded in Paducah. Street workers cleared storm drains before water reached any homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The very stormy weather pattern of March slowly began to change by mid-month. The widespread record-setting rainfall events seen in March were responsible for much of the flooding on the Mississippi River in April. Rainfall was still above normal for April, but excessive rainfall was confined to a few locations where storms trained.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding of the Mississippi River caused a few roads to go underwater, including Highway 123. Highway 123 was closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwest flow brought moisture which overran arctic air just above the surface. Sleet developed during the late morning hours and quickly turned to freezing rain. Initial freezing rain accumulations developed across central Kentucky. Near rush hour...a wide area of freezing rain moved east along and north of Interstate 64. Ice accumulations of over 1/4 inch were widespread. Many accidents were reported during the evening hours. Ice accumulations did not heavily damage trees...but did account for several power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulated to around 1/4 inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front along with a strong upper level low produced a squall line that crossed central Kentucky. This set off widespread severe weather, and spawned several tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado destroyed a trailer and an outbuilding along Hansborough Road. It caused major roof damage along Bethlehem Academy Road, with one home losing the roof completely. Several boats were flipped over, and a large bus was pushed two feet. The tornado pushed in garage doors at an industrial building along Ring Road. This building also suffered significant roof damage. At Central Hardin High School, the tornado snapped concrete pillar stadium lights at the football stadium. The press box was thrown on to the playing field and destroyed. The basketball gym and a wing of the school suffered roof damage consistent with an EF2 tornado. Along Black Branch Road just east of the school complex, the tornado caused some structural damage to homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front along with a strong upper level low produced a squall line that crossed central Kentucky. This set off widespread severe weather, and spawned several tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado knocked a trailer off its foundation at the intersection of Miller Road and Porter Lane. Another trailer near this location was completely destroyed. Another trailer along Miller Road was turned on to its roof.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low pressure tracked from the southern Appalachians to western Virginia by Saturday March 8th. A deepening upper level trough worked into the Ohio Valley overnight, combining with the surface low and strengthening the entire system. The surface pressure gradient tightened in response to the upper low, and gusts of 30 to 40 mph brought blizzard-like conditions to the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to nine inches of snow fell over the county, with the heaviest snow measured in the west.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado touched down on Russell Lane about eight miles east-northeast of Springfield. The tornado completely blew away a large, well-constructed out-building.  Six-by-six inch posts were snapped and piled up, while metal sheeting was blown about a quarter of a mile away. Concrete joists weighing two hundred pounds were displaced.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front along with a strong upper level low produced a squall line that crossed central Kentucky. This set off widespread severe weather, and spawned several tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The first damage occurred two miles west of Carlisle where straight line winds blew the roofs off a barn and a trailer, and a manufacturing company suffered significant roof damage.  Tree and barn damage occurred on KY 32 and KY 1455 north of Carlisle. The most significant damage happened three miles northeast of Carlisle near the intersection of KY 32 and Stoney Creek Road. A small barn was completely destroyed and blown into the highway. Several other barns had their roofs removed. A car was blow 15 feet into a telephone pole by the winds.

The orientation of the wind damage all indicated west to east straight line winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low pressure tracked from the southern Appalachians to western Virginia by Saturday March 8th. A deepening upper level trough worked into the Ohio Valley overnight, combining with the surface low and strengthening the entire system. The surface pressure gradient tightened in response to the upper low, and gusts of 30 to 40 mph brought blizzard-like conditions to the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nine inches of snow was measured in Erlanger. Up to a foot fell across the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front along with a strong upper level low produced a squall line that crossed central Kentucky. This set off widespread severe weather, and spawned several tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado downed numerous hardwood trees on a steep ridge near Elkhorn Creek. It increased in intensity as it moved to the northeast, and did extensive roof damage to two homes on the west side of Lucas Lane. It destroyed five barns on the east side of the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwest flow brought moisture which overran arctic air just above the surface. Sleet developed during the late morning hours and quickly turned to freezing rain. Initial freezing rain accumulations developed across central Kentucky. Near rush hour...a wide area of freezing rain moved east along and north of Interstate 64. Ice accumulations of over 1/4 inch were widespread. Many accidents were reported during the evening hours. Ice accumulations did not heavily damage trees...but did account for several power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulated to around 1/4 inch.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rolling Fork River at Boston crested at 41.2 feet around 345 PM EST on March 20. Flood stage at Boston is 35 feet. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of strong storm systems in February and March combined to raise the Ohio River above flood stage for an extended period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of the Ohio River occurred. At the Evansville, IN gage, the river crested at 43.65 feet on March 23. Flood stage there is 42 feet. Low-lying fields near the river were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of strong storm systems in February and March combined to raise the Ohio River above flood stage for an extended period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of the Ohio River occurred. At the J.T. Myers Lock and Dam gage, the river crested at 48.95 feet on March 26. Flood stage there is 37 feet, and moderate flooding begins at 50 feet. Low-lying fields and some county roads near the river were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of strong storm systems in February and March combined to raise the Ohio River above flood stage for an extended period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding of the Ohio River occurred. At the Paducah gage, the river crested at 45.55 feet on March 28. Flood stage there is 39 feet, and moderate flooding begins at 43 feet. The riverfront park near downtown Paducah was mostly flooded. West of the Interstate 24 bridge, a section of Noble Road was closed by flooding, and water approached several homes. Many acres of agricultural land were flooded. Crop losses were negligible since planting was not yet underway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The very stormy weather pattern of March slowly began to change by mid-month. The widespread record-setting rainfall events seen in March were responsible for much of the flooding on the Ohio River in April. Rainfall was still above normal for April, but excessive rainfall was confined to a few locations where storms trained.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of the Ohio River occurred. At J.T. Myers Dam near Uniontown, the river remained above flood stage from early March through mid April. The crest in April was 45.3 feet on the 11th. Flood stage at Uniontown is 37 feet. Many low-lying areas were flooded, primarily farm fields and woodlands.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The very stormy weather pattern of March slowly began to change by mid-month. The widespread record-setting rainfall events seen in March were responsible for much of the flooding on the Ohio River in April. Rainfall was still above normal for April, but excessive rainfall was confined to a few locations where storms trained.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding of the Ohio River occurred. At Paducah, the river remained above flood stage from mid March through mid April. The crest in April was 45.37 feet on the 11th. Flood stage at Paducah is 39 feet. Many low-lying areas were flooded, primarily farm fields and woodlands. A few secondary roads were closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of strong storm systems in March raised the Mississippi River above flood stage. The record-setting heavy rainfall in southeast Missouri on the 18th and 19th was the primary cause of the flooding.

Flood stage there is 380 feet, and moderate flooding begins at 386 feet. Large areas of bottomland fields and cropland were flooded. Spring planting was not yet underway, so crop losses were negligible.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved northeast along the eastern side of the Appalachian Mountains, spreading a mix of freezing rain, sleet, and snow across the area late of March 7th through March 8th. Several thunderstorms were also reported on the evening of March 7th, with freezing rain and sleet. The precipitation was mainly sleet and freezing rain on the evening of March 7th, but changed over to snow by the morning of March 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four inches of snow fell along Highway 23 at the Virginia state line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system, and its associated cold front, moved across the Ohio Valley on February 17th, bringing strong winds to the region. Several trees were blown down, with some structural damage reported.

Moist and unstable air, combined with daytime heating, allowed for the development of isolated thunderstorms across Eastern Kentucky during the late afternoon and evening hours of July 5th. One of these storms became severe, producing damaging wind gusts.

A squall line moved eastward across southwest Indiana, clipping northwest Kentucky with strong winds and heavy rain. These storms were associated with a 500 mb short wave trough moving east across the mid Mississippi Valley. Sufficient moisture and instability ahead of the line, coupled with strengthening winds aloft, supported forward propagation of the line across the Lower Ohio Valley.

Areas of light freezing rain and freezing drizzle caused slick spots on roadways. Bridges and overpasses were the most affected surfaces. Traffic accidents were not as numerous as during the winter event the previous day. A pickup truck ran off Kentucky Highway 139 in Trigg County and knocked down a power pole. Areas of dense freezing fog contributed to the light icing. Surface high pressure over the southern Great Lakes region provided a cold low level northeast wind flow. At the same time, milder southwest winds just above the surface were overrunning the cold air.

A strong cold front was accompanied by a squall line of thunderstorms with strong winds. Despite record warm temperatures in the 70's, instability was minimal where the narrow squall line occurred. Winds were very strong in the lower levels of the atmosphere. Widespread wind gusts in the 50 to 60 mph range were observed along the squall line. The storms were not prolific lightning producers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An off-duty meteorologist estimated a wind gust to 60 mph. A couple of trees were blown down, blocking roads.

Subfreezing temperatures combined with sparse low level moisture to produce isolated incidences of freezing rain in eastern Kentucky on December 26.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A light glaze of ice was reported on Highway 28 in Talbert.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain fell across portions of Eastern Kentucky late in the afternoon into the evening hours of December 23rd. Light precipitation started out as sleet, freezing rain, and rain during the afternoon hours. By late afternoon, the precipitation had increased in coverage and intensity, and changed over to freezing rain and rain. Arctic air that had been over the area over the previous couple of days allowed for surface temperatures to remain at or below freezing, even as air temperatures rose to near or slightly above freezing. This created very hazardous travel conditions across the area, with numerous accidents reported. Traffic was backed up on the Mountain Parkway due to accidents caused by icy roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain caused a glaze of ice on sidewalks in Eubank.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down in the Benton area.

The heavy wet snow stuck to trees, weighing them down and causing many to snap.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter measured four inches of snow near Irvine.

At the same time, milder southwest winds just above the surface were overrunning the cold air.

Freezing rain fell across portions of Eastern Kentucky late in the afternoon into the evening hours of December 23rd. Light precipitation started out as sleet, freezing rain, and rain during the afternoon hours. By late afternoon, the precipitation had increased in coverage and intensity, and changed over to freezing rain and rain. Arctic air that had been over the area over the previous couple of days allowed for surface temperatures to remain at or below freezing, even as air temperatures rose to near or slightly above freezing. This created very hazardous travel conditions across the area, with numerous accidents reported. Traffic was backed up on the Mountain Parkway due to accidents caused by icy roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous car accidents and road closures were reported due to a glaze of ice from freezing rain in and around Owingsville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds developed ahead of an approaching cold front on December 24th. Winds gusted between 40 and 50 mph at times. These winds were enough to bring down a few trees and tree limbs across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree fell onto a house and several fell across roads and onto power lines.

Strong southerly winds developed ahead of a strong cold front moving east across Missouri. Winds at 2,000 feet were averaging near 60 mph. Enough daytime heating occurred to bring down some of this wind in the afternoon. Gusts at the surface ranged from 45 to 55 mph. The strongest reported wind gust was measured at 55 mph on a hilltop next to the interchange of Interstate 24 and the Purchase Parkway.

The remnants of what once was Tropical Storm Fay moved north from Florida, spreading showers and a few thunderstorms across Eastern Kentucky on August 26th and 27th. A slow moving thunderstorm with heavy rainfall caused flash flooding during the evening hours of August 27th in Letcher County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water was blocking Highways 805 and 317 in northeast Letcher County in the Neon Junction and Haymond areas.

On the afternoon of August 5th, an isolated severe thunderstorm formed in Pike County ahead of a passing middle level trough.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down power lines near Phelps.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving area of moderate to heavy rain caused flooding of roads. Many communities received between one and three inches of rain in the 24 hours leading up to the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was over several roads in Marshall County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving area of moderate to heavy rain caused flooding of roads. Many communities received between one and three inches of rain in the 24 hours leading up to the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At least a couple roads were closed. The Hopkinsville area received 2.64 inches of rain in 12 hours. Extensive flooding of yards occurred along a block-long stretch of a city side street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving slowly south through the area allowed for the development of a few thunderstorms across Eastern Kentucky on September 9th. One of these storms became severe in McCreary County, bringing down several telephone poles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six or seven telephone poles were blown down by thunderstorm wind gusts on Highway 92 East between Route 1044 and Poplar Ford Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system, and its associated cold front, moved across the Ohio Valley region on September 14th. The low pressure system was the remnants of what once was Hurricane Ike. As this weather system approached Eastern Kentucky, winds increased and became strong enough to bring down trees and power lines across portions of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Treess and powelines were blown down countywide.  A trailer near Sandy Hook had much of it's roof blown off.

Areas of freezing rain and freezing drizzle caused roads to become very icy. Numerous accidents were reported, especially on bridges and overpasses. In McCracken County, a dozen wrecks caused major traffic problems. Interstate 24 westbound was closed near exit 11, and U.S. Highway 60 was closed near Barkley Regional Airport. Four people received minor injuries. Kentucky Highway 849 was closed in Graves County, where several cars slid off the road in a few hours. Area schools were closed. The wintry precipitation was caused by a weak surface trough of low pressure combined with a moist southwest flow aloft.

The first winter storm of the season brought widespread sleet and freezing rain. Up to one quarter inch of ice coated roadways during the evening commute. Numerous accidents and slide-offs occurred. An accident on Highway 307 north of Fulton injured a couple of people. Locally higher accumulations of sleet and snow were observed in a small band from Central City westward along the Western Kentucky Parkway. A total of 3.4 inches of sleet and snow fell at Central City. Four inches was reported at Mortons Gap in Hopkins County. A strong cold front ushered much colder air into the region. A weak low pressure system then moved northeast along the front, causing the widespread sleet and freezing rain.

Widespread freezing rain coated surfaces with up to one-tenth inch of ice. Due to very cold antecedent conditions, pavement temperatures were low enough that the initial drops of precipitation immediately created hazardous conditions. A southerly flow of warmer air around high pressure over the Carolina coast overspread a shallow layer of cold air near the surface. The result was widespread icing. The freezing rain occurred during the morning commute, causing a rash of traffic accidents and school closings. Two fatal accidents occurred. In the first fatal wreck, an elderly woman driving on Greenville Road in northern Christian County was unable to stop for a vehicle in front of her. She swerved, went airborne, and struck two trees at the bottom of a ditch. The second fatal accident occurred in Hopkins County on U.S. Highway 62 east of Dawson Springs. A semi-trailer going westbound struck a tree, killing the driver. Across western Kentucky, over one hundred accidents occurred, slowing traffic to a crawl. The Kentucky State Police post that covers the Purchase area (extreme western Kentucky) worked about 100 accidents, including six with injuries. The State Police post based in Madisonville responded to 28 wrecks, including four with injuries. County sheriff departments reported many more additional wrecks, including 30 in McCracken County and 54 in Marshall County. Parts of the Purchase Parkway were closed due to a multi-vehicle pileup involving dozens of cars. Salt trucks and emergency response vehicles slid off roads. Numerous accidents with injuries occurred on U.S. Highway 641 near Murray. Ambulances encountered significant delays in reaching accident victims. In Carlisle County, there were over 50 accidents, including numerous tractor-trailers in ditches or jack-knifed. Major roads such as Kentucky Highway 80 in Graves County were closed due to numerous wrecks and/or disabled vehicles. A few noteworthy wrecks occurred in Henderson County on Highway 41-A, where there was a head-on collision and an overturned vehicle in two unrelated mishaps. Vehicles had difficulty negotiating on and off ramps from freeways. In Henderson, municipal bus service was suspended for several hours. Hospitals in the Paducah area treated a number of persons who slipped and fell on the ice, as well as auto accident victims.

Interstate 64 was closed for a brief time in Boyd County. Conditions improved by late evening, as temperatures rose above freezing.

A strong cold front approached the region during the overnight and early morning hours of February 6th. Widespread wind damage was reported across the area, with trees and power lines blown down, and numerous reports of structural damage. Heavy rain also resulted in flash flooding across the area. Two tornadoes touched down in Eastern Kentucky as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous tree limbs greater than 2 inches torn from trees over a 15 minute period by thunderstorm wind gusts.

Two tornadoes touched down in Eastern Kentucky as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea to nickel sized hail fell in Hyden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow developed during the late afternoon on February 11th and continued until late evening. A swath of 3 to 4 inch accumulations fell across Hancock...Northern Breckenridge...Meade and Jefferson counties eastward along interstate 64 through the northern Bluegrass region. Freezing rain later developed across northern Kentucky during the pre-dawn hours on February 12th. Ice accumulations ranging from 1/4 of an inch to just under 1/2 of an inch were common until temperatures rose above freezing by late morning. Ice accumulations brought minor tree damage. The snow and freezing rain lead to numerous school and activity cancellations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to 4 inches of snow fell the evening of the 11th. Light freezing rain fell early on the 12th.

On Monday, December 15th, a shallow arctic boundary moved into the Tennessee Valley. By late Monday, moist air moved north, overrunning this boundary. A mix of sleet and freezing rain developed along the Ohio River late Monday. A light coating of ice and sleet covered roads  and led to hazardous driving. Early Tuesday, a narrow band of heavy snow formed over central Kentucky. The axis of greatest snow stretched from Ohio County, through Nelson County, and into the northern Bluegrass Region. Two to 5 inches of snow fell overnight and into the early morning hours of Tuesday, Dec 16th. This strip of snow was quite thin, with accumulating snow falling only along a 30 to 40 mile wide swath. Just south of this snow, a mix of freezing rain and sleet brought icy accumulations up to one quarter of an inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to 4 inches of snow, mixed with light amounts of sleet and freezing rain, were measured.

Two tornadoes touched down in Eastern Kentucky as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree was blown down by thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Monday, December 15th, a shallow arctic boundary moved into the Tennessee Valley. By late Monday, moist air moved north, overrunning this boundary. A mix of sleet and freezing rain developed along the Ohio River late Monday. A light coating of ice and sleet covered roads  and led to hazardous driving. Early Tuesday, a narrow band of heavy snow formed over central Kentucky. The axis of greatest snow stretched from Ohio County, through Nelson County, and into the northern Bluegrass Region. Two to 5 inches of snow fell overnight and into the early morning hours of Tuesday, Dec 16th. This strip of snow was quite thin, with accumulating snow falling only along a 30 to 40 mile wide swath. Just south of this snow, a mix of freezing rain and sleet brought icy accumulations up to one quarter of an inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to three inches of snow, mixed with light sleet and freezing rain, fell across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first winter storm of the season brought widespread sleet and freezing rain. Up to one quarter inch of ice coated roadways during the evening commute. Numerous accidents and slide-offs occurred. An accident on Highway 307 north of Fulton injured a couple of people. Locally higher accumulations of sleet and snow were observed in a small band from Central City westward along the Western Kentucky Parkway. A total of 3.4 inches of sleet and snow fell at Central City. Four inches was reported at Mortons Gap in Hopkins County. A strong cold front ushered much colder air into the region. A weak low pressure system then moved northeast along the front, causing the widespread sleet and freezing rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow developed during the late afternoon on February 11th and continued until late evening. A swath of 3 to 4 inch accumulations fell across Hancock...Northern Breckenridge...Meade and Jefferson counties eastward along interstate 64 through the northern Bluegrass region. Freezing rain later developed across northern Kentucky during the pre-dawn hours on February 12th. Ice accumulations ranging from 1/4 of an inch to just under 1/2 of an inch were common until temperatures rose above freezing by late morning. Ice accumulations brought minor tree damage. The snow and freezing rain lead to numerous school and activity cancellations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to 4 inches of snow fell the evening of the 11th across the northern third of the county. Light freezing rain fell early on the 12th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas of freezing rain and freezing drizzle caused roads to become very icy. Numerous accidents were reported, especially on bridges and overpasses. In McCracken County, a dozen wrecks caused major traffic problems. Interstate 24 westbound was closed near exit 11, and U.S. Highway 60 was closed near Barkley Regional Airport. Four people received minor injuries. Kentucky Highway 849 was closed in Graves County, where several cars slid off the road in a few hours. Area schools were closed. The wintry precipitation was caused by a weak surface trough of low pressure combined with a moist southwest flow aloft.

Interstate 64 was closed for a brief time in Boyd County. Conditions improved by late evening, as temperatures rose above freezing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first winter storm of the season brought widespread sleet and freezing rain. Up to one quarter inch of ice coated roadways during the evening commute. Numerous accidents and slide-offs occurred. An accident on Highway 307 north of Fulton injured a couple of people. Widespread freezing rain coated surfaces with up to one-tenth inch of ice. Due to very cold antecedent conditions, pavement temperatures were low enough that the initial drops of precipitation immediately created hazardous conditions. A southerly flow of warmer air around high pressure over the Carolina coast overspread a shallow layer of cold air near the surface. The result was widespread icing. The freezing rain occurred during the morning commute, causing a rash of traffic accidents and school closings. Two fatal accidents occurred. In the first fatal wreck, an elderly woman driving on Greenville Road in northern Christian County was unable to stop for a vehicle in front of her. She swerved, went airborne, and struck two trees at the bottom of a ditch. The second fatal accident occurred in Hopkins County on U.S. Highway 62 east of Dawson Springs. A semi-trailer going westbound struck a tree, killing the driver. Across western Kentucky, over one hundred accidents occurred, slowing traffic to a crawl. The Kentucky State Police post that covers the Purchase area (extreme western Kentucky) worked about 100 accidents, including six with injuries. The State Police post based in Madisonville responded to 28 wrecks, including four with injuries. County sheriff departments reported many more additional wrecks, including 30 in McCracken County and 54 in Marshall County. Parts of the Purchase Parkway were closed due to a multi-vehicle pileup involving dozens of cars. Salt trucks and emergency response vehicles slid off roads. Numerous accidents with injuries occurred on U.S. Highway 641 near Murray. Ambulances encountered significant delays in reaching accident victims. In Carlisle County, there were over 50 accidents, including numerous tractor-trailers in ditches or jack-knifed. Major roads such as Kentucky Highway 80 in Graves County were closed due to numerous wrecks and/or disabled vehicles. A few noteworthy wrecks occurred in Henderson County on Highway 41-A, where there was a head-on collision and an overturned vehicle in two unrelated mishaps. Vehicles had difficulty negotiating on and off ramps from freeways. In Henderson, municipal bus service was suspended for several hours. Hospitals in the Paducah area treated a number of persons who slipped and fell on the ice, as well as auto accident victims.

In the first fatal wreck, an elderly woman driving on Greenville Road in northern Christian County was unable to stop for a vehicle in front of her. She swerved, went airborne, and struck two trees at the bottom of a ditch. The second fatal accident occurred in Hopkins County on U.S. Highway 62 east of Dawson Springs. A semi-trailer going westbound struck a tree, killing the driver. Across western Kentucky, over one hundred accidents occurred, slowing traffic to a crawl. The Kentucky State Police post that covers the Purchase area (extreme western Kentucky) worked about 100 accidents, including six with injuries. The State Police post based in Madisonville responded to 28 wrecks, including four with injuries. County sheriff departments reported many more additional wrecks, including 30 in McCracken County and 54 in Marshall County. Parts of the Purchase Parkway were closed due to a multi-vehicle pileup involving dozens of cars. Salt trucks and emergency response vehicles slid off roads. Numerous accidents with injuries occurred on U.S. Highway 641 near Murray. Ambulances encountered significant delays in reaching accident victims. In Carlisle County, there were over 50 accidents, including numerous tractor-trailers in ditches or jack-knifed. Major roads such as Kentucky Highway 80 in Graves County were closed due to numerous wrecks and/or disabled vehicles. A few noteworthy wrecks occurred in Henderson County on Highway 41-A, where there was a head-on collision and an overturned vehicle in two unrelated mishaps. Vehicles had difficulty negotiating on and off ramps from freeways. In Henderson, municipal bus service was suspended for several hours. Hospitals in the Paducah area treated a number of persons who slipped and fell on the ice, as well as auto accident victims.

The first winter storm of the season brought widespread sleet and freezing rain. Up to one quarter inch of ice coated roadways during the evening commute. Numerous accidents and slide-offs occurred. An accident on Highway 307 north of Fulton injured a couple of people. Locally higher accumulations of sleet and snow were observed in a small band from Central City westward along the Western Kentucky Parkway. A total of 3.4 inches of sleet and snow fell at Central City. Four inches was reported at Mortons Gap in Hopkins County. A strong cold front ushered much colder air into the region. A weak low pressure system then moved northeast along the front, causing the widespread sleet and freezing rain.

A strong cold front approached the region during the overnight and early morning hours of February 6th. Widespread wind damage was reported across the area, with trees and power lines blown down, and numerous reports of structural damage. Heavy rain also resulted in flash flooding across the area. Two tornadoes touched down in Eastern Kentucky as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some trees were blown down by thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Monday, December 15th, a shallow arctic boundary moved into the Tennessee Valley. By late Monday, moist air moved north, overrunning this boundary. A mix of sleet and freezing rain developed along the Ohio River late Monday. A light coating of ice and sleet covered roads  and led to hazardous driving. Early Tuesday, a narrow band of heavy snow formed over central Kentucky. The axis of greatest snow stretched from Ohio County, through Nelson County, and into the northern Bluegrass Region. Two to 5 inches of snow fell overnight and into the early morning hours of Tuesday, Dec 16th. This strip of snow was quite thin, with accumulating snow falling only along a 30 to 40 mile wide swath. Just south of this snow, a mix of freezing rain and sleet brought icy accumulations up to one quarter of an inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to 2 inches of snow fell across the northern portion of the county. Icing of one quarter of an inch was also noted.

Widespread wind damage was reported across the area, with trees and power lines blown down, and numerous reports of structural damage. Heavy rain also resulted in flash flooding across the area. Two tornadoes touched down in Eastern Kentucky as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree and several large branches were blown down along Highway 745 by thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front approached the region during the overnight and early morning hours of February 6th. Widespread wind damage was reported across the area, with trees and power lines blown down, and numerous reports of structural damage. Heavy rain also resulted in flash flooding across the area. Two tornadoes touched down in Eastern Kentucky as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were blown down by thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Monday, December 15th, a shallow arctic boundary moved into the Tennessee Valley. By late Monday, moist air moved north, overrunning this boundary. A mix of sleet and freezing rain developed along the Ohio River late Monday. A light coating of ice and sleet covered roads  and led to hazardous driving. Early Tuesday, a narrow band of heavy snow formed over central Kentucky. The axis of greatest snow stretched from Ohio County, through Nelson County, and into the northern Bluegrass Region. Two to 5 inches of snow fell overnight and into the early morning hours of Tuesday, Dec 16th. This strip of snow was quite thin, with accumulating snow falling only along a 30 to 40 mile wide swath. Just south of this snow, a mix of freezing rain and sleet brought icy accumulations up to one quarter of an inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to 3 inches of snow, mixed with light sleet and freezing rain, fell across the northern portion of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front approached the region during the overnight and early morning hours of February 6th. Widespread wind damage was reported across the area, with trees and power lines blown down, and numerous reports of structural damage. Heavy rain also resulted in flash flooding across the area. Two tornadoes touched down in Eastern Kentucky as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pompeii road was closed due to flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow developed during the late afternoon on February 11th and continued until late evening. A swath of 3 to 4 inch accumulations fell across Hancock...Northern Breckenridge...Meade and Jefferson counties eastward along interstate 64 through the northern Bluegrass region. Freezing rain later developed across northern Kentucky during the pre-dawn hours on February 12th. Ice accumulations ranging from 1/4 of an inch to just under 1/2 of an inch were common until temperatures rose above freezing by late morning. Ice accumulations brought minor tree damage. The snow and freezing rain lead to numerous school and activity cancellations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to 4 inches of snow fell across the southern part of the county. Light freezing rain fell during the early hours on the 12th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas of freezing rain and freezing drizzle caused roads to become very icy. Numerous accidents were reported, especially on bridges and overpasses. In McCracken County, a dozen wrecks caused major traffic problems. Interstate 24 westbound was closed near exit 11, and U.S. Highway 60 was closed near Barkley Regional Airport. Four people received minor injuries. Kentucky Highway 849 was closed in Graves County, where several cars slid off the road in a few hours. Area schools were closed. The wintry precipitation was caused by a weak surface trough of low pressure combined with a moist southwest flow aloft.

Widespread freezing rain coated surfaces with up to one-tenth inch of ice. Due to very cold antecedent conditions, pavement temperatures were low enough that the initial drops of precipitation immediately created hazardous conditions. A southerly flow of warmer air around high pressure over the Carolina coast overspread a shallow layer of cold air near the surface. The result was widespread icing. The freezing rain occurred during the morning commute, causing a rash of traffic accidents and school closings. Two fatal accidents occurred. In the first fatal wreck, an elderly woman driving on Greenville Road in northern Christian County was unable to stop for a vehicle in front of her. She swerved, went airborne, and struck two trees at the bottom of a ditch. The second fatal accident occurred in Hopkins County on U.S. Highway 62 east of Dawson Springs. A semi-trailer going westbound struck a tree, killing the driver. Across western Kentucky, over one hundred accidents occurred, slowing traffic to a crawl. The Kentucky State Police post that covers the Purchase area (extreme western Kentucky) worked about 100 accidents, including six with injuries. The State Police post based in Madisonville responded to 28 wrecks, including four with injuries. County sheriff departments reported many more additional wrecks, including 30 in McCracken County and 54 in Marshall County. Parts of the Purchase Parkway were closed due to a multi-vehicle pileup involving dozens of cars. Salt trucks and emergency response vehicles slid off roads. Numerous accidents with injuries occurred on U.S. Highway 641 near Murray. Ambulances encountered significant delays in reaching accident victims. In Carlisle County, there were over 50 accidents, including numerous tractor-trailers in ditches or jack-knifed. Major roads such as Kentucky Highway 80 in Graves County were closed due to numerous wrecks and/or disabled vehicles. A few noteworthy wrecks occurred in Henderson County on Highway 41-A, where there was a head-on collision and an overturned vehicle in two unrelated mishaps. Vehicles had difficulty negotiating on and off ramps from freeways. In Henderson, municipal bus service was suspended for several hours. Hospitals in the Paducah area treated a number of persons who slipped and fell on the ice, as well as auto accident victims.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain began as freezing rain late in the afternoon on the 23rd. Air temperatures were in the upper 20s and lower 30s.  The ground was cold due to the temperatures in the single digits and teens on the 22nd.||A light ice coating, well under a quarter of an inch, formed for the late afternoon commute home. ||Numerous minor vehicular accidents were reported.  Interstate 64 was closed for a brief time in Boyd County. Conditions improved by late evening, as temperatures rose above freezing.

The first winter storm of the season brought widespread sleet and freezing rain. Up to one quarter inch of ice coated roadways during the evening commute. Numerous accidents and slide-offs occurred. An accident on Highway 307 north of Fulton injured a couple of people. Locally higher accumulations of sleet and snow were observed in a small band from Central City westward along the Western Kentucky Parkway. A total of 3.4 inches of sleet and snow fell at Central City. Four inches was reported at Mortons Gap in Hopkins County. A strong cold front ushered much colder air into the region. A weak low pressure system then moved northeast along the front, causing the widespread sleet and freezing rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Monday, December 15th, a shallow arctic boundary moved into the Tennessee Valley. By late Monday, moist air moved north, overrunning this boundary. A mix of sleet and freezing rain developed along the Ohio River late Monday. A light coating of ice and sleet covered roads  and led to hazardous driving. Early Tuesday, a narrow band of heavy snow formed over central Kentucky. The axis of greatest snow stretched from Ohio County, through Nelson County, and into the northern Bluegrass Region. Two to 5 inches of snow fell overnight and into the early morning hours of Tuesday, Dec 16th. This strip of snow was quite thin, with accumulating snow falling only along a 30 to 40 mile wide swath. Just south of this snow, a mix of freezing rain and sleet brought icy accumulations up to one quarter of an inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three and one half inches of snow accumulated near Carlisle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow developed during the late afternoon on February 11th and continued until late evening. A swath of 3 to 4 inch accumulations fell across Hancock...Northern Breckenridge...Meade and Jefferson counties eastward along interstate 64 through the northern Bluegrass region. Freezing rain later developed across northern Kentucky during the pre-dawn hours on February 12th. Ice accumulations ranging from 1/4 of an inch to just under 1/2 of an inch were common until temperatures rose above freezing by late morning. Ice accumulations brought minor tree damage. The snow and freezing rain lead to numerous school and activity cancellations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to 4 inches of snow fell across northern parts of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain developed during the afternoon of December 23rd. Ice accumulation on roads across the northern portions of Kentucky lead to numerous traffic accidents and several fatalities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slick roads due to light freezing rain lead to several injury-causing accidents and one fatality in the Louisville metropolitan area. The fatality occurred when a driver lot control of his vehicle and in eastern Jefferson County. Another accident on the Gene Snyder Expressway injured  two emergency workers who were providing aid to a driver hurt in an earlier crash. The three were taken to University Hospital with injuries that did not appear to be life-threatening. Emergency workers in Louisville responded to as many as 40 calls about accidents between 2 and 5 p.m due to the icy conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain developed during the afternoon of December 23rd. Ice accumulation on roads across the northern portions of Kentucky lead to numerous traffic accidents and several fatalities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Icy roads lead to a  fatality on Kentucky Hwy 86 near Garfield. A teenager who had stopped with her father to help another motorist was killed by a hit and run driver on the icy roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain developed during the afternoon of December 23rd. Ice accumulation on roads across the northern portions of Kentucky lead to numerous traffic accidents and several fatalities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain brought a rash of accidents on highways around the Lexington area. Broadcast media reported at least 50 accidents from early afternoon into the evening. Interstates 71, 75 and the Interstate 275 loop were reported as virtually impassable. An out-of-state driver was killed on Interstate 75 in Lexington when his vehicle was struck by another.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain developed during the afternoon of December 23rd. Ice accumulation on roads across the northern portions of Kentucky lead to numerous traffic accidents and several fatalities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: US Highway 25 was briefly closed between Georgetown and Sadieville due to icy conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwest flow brought moisture which overran arctic air just above the surface. Sleet developed during the late morning hours and quickly turned to freezing rain. Initial freezing rain accumulations developed across central Kentucky. Near rush hour...a wide area of freezing rain moved east along and north of Interstate 64. Ice accumulations of over 1/4 inch were widespread. Many accidents were reported during the evening hours. Ice accumulations did not heavily damage trees...but did account for several power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Icing of around one quarter of an inch was common.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brief period of high winds followed in the wake of a cold front passage. The most intense damage occurred in Hickman in Fulton County, where estimated winds of 90 mph occurred. The bulk of the damage occurred in downtown Hickman and two blocks west, along a bluff over the Mississippi River. Two multi-level brick buildings over 100 years old received major damage. The roof was stripped off an historic hotel, and the entire face of the second building crumbled. The canopies of a gas station and another building were destroyed. The second story porch of a home atop the bluff was destroyed. There was widespread roof shingle damage, street sign damage, and tree damage. A few vehicles were damaged or destroyed by falling debris. A couple of houses were hit by falling trees. An old hardware store suffered major damage. At one point, 11 city streets, six county roads, and six state highways were partially or totally blocked by debris. The largest magnolia tree in the state fell. In Murray, a tree was blown over. The tree landed on several cars.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: One quarter inch of ice knocked out power to 1900 customers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across Eastern Kentucky during the early morning hours of January 11th. Severe thunderstorms developed out ahead of, and along the front. These storms began to affect Eastern Kentucky late in the afternoon of January 10th, and continued periodically through the early morning of January 11th. Several trees were reported down across portions of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trees were blown down on Lower Craney Road by wind gusts estimated at 61 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful cold front set off a lengthy squall line that crossed all of  central Kentucky on the evening of January 29. A large number of locations had 60 to as much as 100 mph winds, causing extensive property damage.  There were also a few small tornado spin-ups.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds destroyed an older barn and heavily damaged a newer one to the west of Campbellsville. In the Mannsville area, straight line winds caused moderate to severe damage to a business.

A powerful cold front moved rapidly southeast across western Kentucky during the late afternoon hours. An organized line of severe thunderstorms developed along the front as it crossed southeast Missouri, then swept east across western Kentucky. Widespread damaging winds accompanied the line of storms. Temperatures fell about 30 degrees in less than one hour when the very strong cold front passed through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Fulton, some shingles were blown from roofs. A few trees were blown down.

Part of the roof of a new storage building was blown off. Power lines were down. A wind gust to 69 mph was measured at the Bardwell Fire Department and County Rescue Squad building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful cold front set off a lengthy squall line that crossed all of  central Kentucky on the evening of January 29. A large number of locations had 60 to as much as 100 mph winds, causing extensive property damage.  There were also a few small tornado spin-ups.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fast moving EF-1 tornado briefly touched down four times in the Louisville Metro area as a squall line crossed the city. The tornado was on the ground for approximately 1.5 miles over the course of it's 16 mile long track.  The first touchdown was in and industrial area just off Millers Lane west of the Dixie Highway. The tornado stayed on the ground for one mile before lifting, heavily damaging a church on Dixie Highway, as well as uprooting and snapping several trees and damaging numerous homes.  The tornado then dipped to earth again on the west side of the University of Louisville campus, breaking out many windows in a large residence hall and nearby building, and damaging several vehicles parked nearby.  The next touchdown happened in Saint Matthews near the intersection of Shelbyville Road and Interstate 264, where extensive damage was suffered by many businesses and private properties.  The fourth and final touchdown was in Anchorage where many trees were damaged, blown over, and uprooted, roofs were damaged, and a large outbuilding at a training school was destroyed.  Widespread downburst wind damage occurred mainly to the south of the tornado's path as the squall line moved across the city.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front along with a strong upper level low produced a squall line that crossed central Kentucky. This set off widespread severe weather, and spawned several tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado first touched down near Lone Star, where it destroyed several barns and a mobile home on or near Lone Star Road. The tornado next touched down along U.S. Highway 31W north of Bonnieville, where it heavily damaged or destroyed several more barns or mobile homes. It touched down again near the intersection of Hammonville and Bacon Creek Roads. Here, it caused minor damage to some homes, destroyed a barn, and damaged the roof of another. The tornado continued on eastward, where it damaged several barns near U.S. Highway 31E.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front along with a strong upper level low produced a squall line that crossed central Kentucky. This set off widespread severe weather, and spawned several tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most of the damage caused by this tornado was concentrated in the Flood Road area, as it intensified while moving to the northeast. The tornado destroyed a large, well built barn, and threw the structure 50 yards. It moved an 18 thousand pound trailer four feet, and flipped it. Another barn was destroyed, and every shingle was blown off a well constructed roof top. Several other homes suffered some degree of roof damage. Besides the structural damage, around 40 large hardwood trees were uprooted along the path.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front along with a strong upper level low produced a squall line that crossed central Kentucky. This set off widespread severe weather, and spawned several tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado destroyed two barns and caused substantial damage to three others. A house was damaged, and a corn crib was destroyed. Many trees were uprooted and a few were snapped.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A path  of straight line wind damage occurred along and after the path of an associated tornado southeast of Park city. The most significant damage was to a mobile home that was destroyed when thunderstorm winds blew in the back wall and ripped the roof off of it on Gateway Court. Three occupants were covered by a collapsing wall, but were unhurt. Four barns were destroyed or severely damaged in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through Kentucky during the evening of January 29, 2008, into the overnight hours of January 30th. A line of severe thunderstorms accompanied the front, resulting in widespread wind damage across Eastern Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Road Dept responded to reports of approximately 25 trees blocking roads all across the county. Highway 3190 was still blocked by downed trees as of 230 pm EST. This damage was caused by wind gusts estimated at 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through Kentucky during the evening of January 29, 2008, into the overnight hours of January 30th. A line of severe thunderstorms accompanied the front, resulting in widespread wind damage across Eastern Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Approximately 10 trees were blown down and blocking roads. This damage was caused by wind gusts estimated at 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful cold front set off a lengthy squall line that crossed all of  central Kentucky on the evening of January 29. A large number of locations had 60 to as much as 100 mph winds, causing extensive property damage.  There were also a few small tornado spin-ups.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down two walls at an apartment complex in the Cave City area. Winds blew the roof off a home in the Capitol Hill communitiy near Glasgow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful cold front set off a lengthy squall line that crossed all of  central Kentucky on the evening of January 29. A large number of locations had 60 to as much as 100 mph winds, causing extensive property damage.  There were also a few small tornado spin-ups.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed trees and a power pole.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front along with a strong upper level low produced a squall line that crossed central Kentucky. This set off widespread severe weather, and spawned several tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down near 812 Mt. Vernon Road, damaging a home and a barn at that location. Along Connersville Road, the tornado lifted the roofs off three homes and damaged three others. It also destroyed several barns and outbuildings, and snapped numerous hardwood trees. It damaged a few more barns and trees before lifting about a half mile southwest of Cynthiana. Several businesses in the city of Cynthiana had sign damage from 70 mph straight line winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front along with a strong upper level low produced a squall line that crossed central Kentucky. This set off widespread severe weather, and spawned several tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down on Pleasant Drive about two miles southeast of Springfield. It was embedded within an area of straight-line wind damage. The tornado blew the roof off a house, and threw it about 125 yards downwind.  Several pine trees were snapped and small outbuildings were destroyed.  A 14 inch diameter pine tree was snapped and moved about 45 yards downwind as well.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed trees and blew a dumpster across a road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening low pressure system moved northeast from Arkansas along a warm front that extended across southeast Missouri and southern Illinois. Surface dew points were in the upper 50's to lower 60's along and southeast of the warm front. A strong upper level trough over the western Plains produced a strong southwest flow of 60 to 80 knots at 500 mb. Winds at 850 mb were south to southwest around 40 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twenty-seven homes were damaged and 17 were destroyed. A total of 19 garages, sheds, and barns were damaged or destroyed. Of the destroyed homes, at least five were mobile homes. The two injured persons transported themselves to local hospitals. The county was declared a federal major disaster area. Peak winds were estimated near 130 mph. The average path width was 225 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful cold front set off a lengthy squall line that crossed all of  central Kentucky on the evening of January 29. A large number of locations had 60 to as much as 100 mph winds, causing extensive property damage.  There were also a few small tornado spin-ups.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed at least 20 tobacco barns and other outbuildings. At least one home was damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure organized over Louisiana, spreading precipitation northeast along a warm front that extended into the Ohio Valley. Arctic high pressure over the Great Lakes region produced a cold northeast wind flow near the surface. This shallow cold air was responsible for freezing rain. Although this was the second ice storm in only ten days, ice accumulations were much less serious than the crippling ice storm on February 11. One-quarter to one-half inch of ice accumulated in the Ohio River counties from Paducah to Owensboro, as well as Ballard, Hopkins, and Webster Counties. Many schools and businesses were closed for this second round of ice. A tree fell on a power line near Madisonville, knocking out power to a couple hundred homes. Numerous tree limbs fell into power lines in Madisonville. Kentucky State Police worked eight wrecks in Hopkins County, mostly on the Western Kentucky and Pennyrile Parkways. Hopkins County and Madisonville law enforcement agencies worked about 20 accidents. Although less ice accumulated to the south and east, traffic problems were also widespread. Interstate 24 was closed both east and westbound in the Hopkinsville area, where dozens of accidents occurred. Local hospitals treated four people for injuries due to slips, falls, and accidents. The Western Kentucky Parkway was littered with cars off the side of the road. The main effect of this winter storm was to slow down cleanup efforts following the major ice storm on Feb. 11.

A slow moving cold front approached the area during the afternoon hours of April 11th. Severe thunderstorms developed along and ahead of the front. The storms first moved into Eastern Kentucky shortly before 2 PM EDT. These storms affected the areas near Monticello and Somerset. A tornado was confirmed to have touched down in Wayne County. These and other storms moved northeast across the area, causing wind damage. Numerous reports of structural damage and downed trees were reported across portions of the area. Large hail was also reported with some of the storms. Very heavy rainfall with some of the storms caused flash flooding in portions of Laurel County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A barn and many trees were blown down by thunderstorm wind gusts.

Gusty winds behind a strong cold front caused some damage across Eastern Kentucky during the late afternoon and early evening hours of February 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down across Liberty Road 3 miles northwest of West Liberty by non-thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front approached the region during the overnight and early morning hours of February 6th. Widespread wind damage was reported across the area, with trees and power lines blown down, and numerous reports of structural damage. Heavy rain also resulted in flash flooding across the area. Two tornadoes touched down in Eastern Kentucky as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two large trees were blown down along Highway 11; one of the trees fell onto some powerlines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong south winds gusted from 45 to 50 mph. The peak wind gust was 47 mph at Paducah, 46 mph at Hopkinsville, and 45 mph at Owensboro. Shingles were blown off some roofs in the Owensboro area. The strong winds occurred in advance of a strong cold front that moved across southern Illinois during the late afternoon. The strong winds brought down some tree limbs that were laden with ice from the February 11 ice storm. This caused a setback in efforts to restore power following the ice storm.

A strong synoptic low passed over Ohio on January 1st. Snowfall started in the wrap around moisture with this system, and by daybreak of the 2nd there was significant blowing and drifting that made the snow difficult to measure.||During the daytime hours of January 1st, a lake effect snow band set up through Richmond and southern Dayton to Wilmington. Scattered snow showers were found throughout the region through the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch of snow was measured in Walton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong south winds gusted from 45 to 50 mph. The peak wind gust was 47 mph at Paducah, 46 mph at Hopkinsville, and 45 mph at Owensboro. Shingles were blown off some roofs in the Owensboro area. The strong winds occurred in advance of a strong cold front that moved across southern Illinois during the late afternoon. The strong winds brought down some tree limbs that were laden with ice from the February 11 ice storm. This caused a setback in efforts to restore power following the ice storm.

Low pressure organized over Louisiana, spreading precipitation northeast along a warm front that extended into the Ohio Valley. Arctic high pressure over the Great Lakes region produced a cold northeast wind flow near the surface. This shallow cold air was responsible for freezing rain. Although this was the second ice storm in only ten days, ice accumulations were much less serious than the crippling ice storm on February 11. One-quarter to one-half inch of ice accumulated in the Ohio River counties from Paducah to Owensboro, as well as Ballard, Hopkins, and Webster Counties. Many schools and businesses were closed for this second round of ice. A tree fell on a power line near Madisonville, knocking out power to a couple hundred homes. Numerous tree limbs fell into power lines in Madisonville. Kentucky State Police worked eight wrecks in Hopkins County, mostly on the Western Kentucky and Pennyrile Parkways. Hopkins County and Madisonville law enforcement agencies worked about 20 accidents. Although less ice accumulated to the south and east, traffic problems were also widespread. Interstate 24 was closed both east and westbound in the Hopkinsville area, where dozens of accidents occurred. Local hospitals treated four people for injuries due to slips, falls, and accidents. The Western Kentucky Parkway was littered with cars off the side of the road. The main effect of this winter storm was to slow down cleanup efforts following the major ice storm on Feb. 11.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful cold front moved rapidly southeast across western Kentucky during the late afternoon hours. An organized line of severe thunderstorms developed along the front as it crossed southeast Missouri, then swept east across western Kentucky. Widespread damaging winds accompanied the line of storms. Temperatures fell about 30 degrees in less than one hour when the very strong cold front passed through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down countywide. Minor structural damage occurred, mainly to roofs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful cold front moved rapidly southeast across western Kentucky during the late afternoon hours. An organized line of severe thunderstorms developed along the front as it crossed southeast Missouri, then swept east across western Kentucky. Widespread damaging winds accompanied the line of storms. Temperatures fell about 30 degrees in less than one hour when the very strong cold front passed through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At a shopping mall in Hopkinsville, glass windows were blown out of a department store. A woman was injured when a flying glass pane struck her in the back. She was treated at a hospital for cuts to her back. Five tractor-trailer rigs were blown over, including one blown over on Interstate 24. One of the trucks was a fuel tanker that was leaking fuel. Widespread tree damage was reported from Crofton through Hopkinsville. Hundreds of trees were blown down, despite not being in leaf. At least a few cars were damaged by falling trees. At an industrial park, two storage buildings sustained roof damage. Another building collapsed. Several utility poles were snapped. Lots of shingles were ripped off roofs. A couple of mobile homes were damaged. A commercial building sign was blown over. A wind gust to 69 mph was measured at the Fort Campbell weather station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening low pressure system moved northeast from Arkansas along a warm front that extended across southeast Missouri and southern Illinois. Surface dew points were in the upper 50's to lower 60's along and southeast of the warm front. A strong upper level trough over the western Plains produced a strong southwest flow of 60 to 80 knots at 500 mb. Winds at 850 mb were south to southwest around 40 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail occurred on Highway 166.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three and a half inches of snow fell in Maysville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong wind gusts were reported across portions of Eastern Kentucky during the overnight and daytime hours of February 10th. These damaging winds occurred as strong winds aloft were brought down to the surface as a cold front moved across the area. Several trees were blown down across portions of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree was blown down on U.S. Highway 460 one quarter of a mile from the Millard Grade School or 6 miles northwest of Belcher.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty winds ahead of an approaching low pressure system occurred across a portion of Eastern Kentucky on February 5th. These winds were strong enough to produce isolated damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down county wide between 9 pm and midnight. The worst damaged appeared to have occurred in the western portion of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front along with a strong upper level low produced a squall line that crossed central Kentucky. This set off widespread severe weather, and spawned several tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado destroyed 12 homes and mobile homes, mainly in the Amos community and in the Tracy Lane area. Many other homes and buildings were damaged. Four people were killed in the Tracy Lane area, and eleven others were injured in southeast Allen County. The tornado continued through rural and wooded sections of eastern Allen County, and crossed into Monroe County, Kentucky near the town of Fountain Run.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow developed during the late afternoon on February 11th and continued until late evening. A swath of 3 to 4 inch accumulations fell across Hancock...Northern Breckenridge...Meade and Jefferson counties eastward along interstate 64 through the northern Bluegrass region. Freezing rain later developed across northern Kentucky during the pre-dawn hours on February 12th. Ice accumulations ranging from 1/4 of an inch to just under 1/2 of an inch were common until temperatures rose above freezing by late morning. Ice accumulations brought minor tree damage. The snow and freezing rain lead to numerous school and activity cancellations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to 4 inches of snow fell the evening of the 11th. One quarter of an inch of ice coated trees the morning of the 12th. Several thousand residents lost power due to ice covered branches falling on power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwest flow brought moisture which overran arctic air just above the surface. Sleet developed during the late morning hours and quickly turned to freezing rain. Initial freezing rain accumulations developed across central Kentucky. Near rush hour...a wide area of freezing rain moved east along and north of Interstate 64. Ice accumulations of over 1/4 inch were widespread. Many accidents were reported during the evening hours. Ice accumulations did not heavily damage trees...but did account for several power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulations amounted to near one quarter of an inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty winds ahead of an approaching low pressure system occurred across a portion of Eastern Kentucky during the overnight and predawn hours of February 6th. These winds were strong enough to produce isolated damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down county wide. Some of the downed trees were blocking area roads. Power lines were also blown down in the Revelo area and on Cabin Creek Road in Stearns.
