EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Training thunderstorms brought heavy rains and flash flooding to parts of central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scott Hill Road was closed due to high water. Knob Road and Pine Ridge Road near Battletown were closed due to high water. Some roads near Rhodelia were covered with water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain rates of around a half inch in less than 3 hours occurred just before dawn on Friday, the 4th.  This was out ahead of an approaching cold front.  Prior to those heavier showers, widespread rain fell on Thursday, the 3rd, from a warm front.  The total rainfall in less than 36 hours was 2 to 3 inches across northeast Kentucky.  Specifically, Warnock measured 2.99 inches, Olive Hill had 2.56 inches, Ashland reported 2.3 inches, and Charley observed 2.22 inches.||Minor small stream flooding was widespread on the morning of the 4th.  A few of the specific streams included the headwaters of Blaine Creek in Lawrence County, around Martha and Blaine.  Barrett Creek flooded an area west of Grayson in Carter County.  In Boyd County, Hoods Creek in Westwood, and Williams Creek near Princess flooded.  Tygarts Creek in Greenup County rose 15 feet in the vertical near Warnock, flooding fields.||Numerous roads were closed across northeast Kentucky.  However, no structural damage was noted.

Cold air above the surface of the ground, combined with showers and isolated thunderstorms moving across the area, resulted in a report of large hail in Southern Kentucky on April 28th. Nickel size hail was reported in Pulaski county during the early evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel sized hail was reported near Bronston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving cold front coupled with an upper level low brought some heavy rains and a few severe thunderstorms to parts of north central and east central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rolling Fork River at Boston crested at 37.6 feet around 7 AM EST on March 6. Flood stage at Boston is 35 feet. Minor flooding occurs at this level, with some agricultural bottom land covered with water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving cold front coupled with an upper level low brought some heavy rains and a few severe thunderstorms to parts of north central and east central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Green River at Woodbury crested at 36.7 feet around 5 AM CST on March 6. Flood stage at Woodbury is 33 feet. Minor flooding occurs at this level with some lowlands around the town of Woodbury covered with water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front along with a strong upper level low produced a squall line that crossed central Kentucky. This set off widespread severe weather, and spawned several tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This EF3 tornado crossed into a wooded section of Monroe County from Allen County, and lifted near Highway 87 south of the town of Fountain Run. Several outbuildings were destroyed and a car was flipped on Circle D Lane. On Akersville Road, fences were downed and about 200,000 log feet of timber were knocked down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving cold front coupled with an upper level low brought some heavy rains and a few severe thunderstorms to parts of north central and east central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Green River at Rochester crested at 19.3 feet around 405 PM CST on March 6. Flood stage at Rochester is 17 feet. Minor flooding occurs at this level, with ferry operations shut down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving cold front coupled with an upper level low brought some heavy rains and a few severe thunderstorms to parts of north central and east central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rough River at Dundee crested at 27.4 feet around 1130 AM CST on March 5. Flood stage at Dundee is 25 feet. Minor flooding occurs at this level, with a half-mile section of Kentucky Highway 69 flooded near the bridge. Bottom land begins to flood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving cold front coupled with an upper level low brought some heavy rains and a few severe thunderstorms to parts of north central and east central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gilbert Creek Road, Dry Dock Road, and Rice Road were all covered by flowing water in spots due to streams flowing out of their banks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving cold front coupled with an upper level low brought some heavy rains and a few severe thunderstorms to parts of north central and east central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Old Brownsboro Road Alternate near the intersection of Highway 329 and Interstate 71 was covered by a foot of flowing water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving cold front coupled with an upper level low brought some heavy rains and a few severe thunderstorms to parts of north central and east central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed a tree, and the tree fell through a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving cold front coupled with an upper level low brought some heavy rains and a few severe thunderstorms to parts of north central and east central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed trees.

Cold northwest flow behind a departing storm system, combined with moisture off Lake Michigan, brought widespread snowfall to eastern Kentucky on February 27. Most lower elevation locations received 1 to 2 inches of snow, with localized 3 inch amounts. However, the higher terrain along the Virginia border receieved between 5 and 8 inches of snow, as upslope winds interacting with the terrain helped enhance snowfall amounts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A coop observer measured 8 inches of snowfall on Black Mountain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of strong storm systems in February and March combined to raise the Ohio River above flood stage for an extended period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of the Ohio River occurred. At the Smithland gage, the river crested at 45.17 feet on March 28. Flood stage there is 40 feet, and moderate flooding begins at 48 feet. Parts of Kentucky 137 and 1436 were closed, affecting the communities of Birdsville and Bayou. Agricultural land and wooded river bottomlands were flooded. Crop losses were negligible since planting was not yet underway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow developed during the late afternoon on February 11th and continued until late evening. A swath of 3 to 4 inch accumulations fell across Hancock...Northern Breckenridge...Meade and Jefferson counties eastward along interstate 64 through the northern Bluegrass region. Freezing rain later developed across northern Kentucky during the pre-dawn hours on February 12th. Ice accumulations ranging from 1/4 of an inch to just under 1/2 of an inch were common until temperatures rose above freezing by late morning. Ice accumulations brought minor tree damage. The snow and freezing rain lead to numerous school and activity cancellations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to 4 inches of snow fell during the evening of the 11th. Freezing rain early on the 12th coated trees. A branch falling on a power line in Frankfort caused a power outage to several neighborhoods.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful cold front moved rapidly southeast across western Kentucky during the late afternoon hours. An organized line of severe thunderstorms developed along the front as it crossed southeast Missouri, then swept east across western Kentucky. Widespread damaging winds accompanied the line of storms. Temperatures fell about 30 degrees in less than one hour when the very strong cold front passed through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down in the city and surrounding county roads. Some bricks were blown off buildings.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A power pole fell across U.S. Highway 51, blocking traffic. A tree fell across the same highway near mile marker 14.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front acted as a focus for some heavy rains over parts of central Kentucky. Along with this, an upper level system set off some severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado first touched down along Millstown Road southwest of Park City. It uprooted and snapped the trunks of several large trees, and did some minor roof damage to a nearby home. The tornado was on the ground intermittently along its path to the south and east of Park City, destroying eight barns and uprooting or snapping numerous trees. Right before it lifted, it tore the front porch off a home and destroyed a barn. A path of straight line wind damage continued for around a mile and a half after the tornado lifted.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel was reported with rotation along Highway 161. A wall cloud was also observed.

A low pressure system developed over the central Gulf coast, then moved northeast across the Tennessee Valley. This surface low produced light to moderate accumulations of snow early in the morning. Following the surface low, an intensifying upper level low produced a more significant round of heavy snow during the late afternoon and nighttime hours. Four to eight inches of snow fell along and southeast of a line from Morganfield (Union County) through Princeton (Caldwell County) and Benton (Marshall County) to Murray (Calloway County). The highest amounts of 6 to 8 inches were over the Pennyrile region, including Owensboro, Henderson, Madisonville, and Hopkinsville. Isolated amounts to 10 inches were reported in Muhlenberg County. Gusty winds caused by a strong pressure gradient between the surface low and arctic high pressure over the upper Mississippi Valley caused blowing and drifting snow. Visibility was below one-quarter mile at times. Roads became snow-covered and very slippery, and a number of accidents occurred. A major wreck involved two tractor-trailer rigs on the Pennyrile Parkway in Henderson County. Three people were injured, none seriously. The northbound lanes of the parkway were shut down. About a dozen other accidents were reported in Henderson County. Drifts were commonly measured around one foot deep, with isolated reports of 2-foot drifts. Schools were closed.

A cold front along with a strong upper level low produced a squall line that crossed central Kentucky. This set off widespread severe weather, and spawned several tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down on the east side of Harrodsburg and collapsed three walls of a warehouse. Near the intersection of KY 152 and Mac Court extensive tree damage occurred.  Metal sheeting was found in trees. A large tree fell on a house and power lines, and that same house had its garage destroyed. A small branch was driven into the side of a car and a chimney was knocked down from a house. Near KY 152 and Montrose Street the roof of a factory was blown in and extensive tree damage occurred. An elementary school suffered extensive roof and ceiling damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front along with a strong upper level low produced a squall line that crossed central Kentucky. This set off widespread severe weather, and spawned several tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado caused extensive tree and power line damage near Happy Ridge Road, Wood Dale Road, and Maple Grove Drive. Several businesses were damaged in the Broadway and Lakeview Drive area. A cinder block storage building was toppled. The tornado knocked down the exterior wall of another building. A storage building was lifted up and thrown across Broadway. The Old Brandenburg Telephone Company Office had roof damage. A church was damaged near the intersection of Broadway and Highway 933.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front along with a strong upper level low produced a squall line that crossed central Kentucky. This set off widespread severe weather, and spawned several tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew a tree on to a trailer destroyed on Pike Road. Power lines were downed on Pyles Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed over the southern Plains, spreading widespread heavy precipitation across western Kentucky. At the same time, high pressure over the upper Ohio Valley produced a cold easterly wind flow. The result was a crippling ice storm. Around one inch of ice caused extensive damage across parts of western Kentucky, along and north of a line from Paducah to Madisonville. Many of those same areas received two to four inches of sleet and snow. Numerous trees and power lines were brought down, knocking out power to many thousands of homes. Power outages lasted up to a week. Emergency shelters were established in several counties (including Livingston, Crittenden, and Hopkins Counties) for those without power for extended periods. The National Guard was mobilized in Crittenden and Livingston Counties to help conduct door-to-door welfare checks and direct traffic. Many roads were covered by downed trees and tree limbs. Even some major roads such as U.S. Highway 60 were closed or impassable for over 24 hours in Livingston and Crittenden Counties. Travel there was banned for all but emergency traffic. Near Paducah, a fatal accident occurred shortly after the onset of freezing rain (indirect fatality). A woman lost control of her SUV in a curve and hit a tree at 45 mph. That wreck was one of 14 weather-related crashes in McCracken County. A section of the Western Kentucky Parkway was closed from the Pennyrile Parkway to Dawson Springs while tree debris was cleared from the road. All roads into and out of Dawson Springs were blocked by trees at one point.

A deepening low pressure system moved northeast from Arkansas along a warm front that extended across southeast Missouri and southern Illinois. Surface dew points were in the upper 50's to lower 60's along and southeast of the warm front. A strong upper level trough over the western Plains produced a strong southwest flow of 60 to 80 knots at 500 mb. Winds at 850 mb were south to southwest around 40 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large metal awning was wrapped around the front of a restaurant. A tree was split nearby.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half of a roof, including the trusses, were torn off and tossed to the east-northeast.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Milburn Baptist Church was damaged. Sections of roof were blown off of two homes along State Route 1371. A tree landed on the roof of a home along County Road 1120. A downed tree blocked both lanes of State Route 80 east.

A strong upper level trough over the western Plains produced a strong southwest flow of 60 to 80 knots at 500 mb. Winds at 850 mb were south to southwest around 40 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One mobile home suffered severe damage. The roof was peeled off a second mobile home. A nearby vehicle had a 2x4 impaled into one side.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of strong storm systems in February and March combined to raise the Ohio River above flood stage for an extended period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of the Ohio River occurred. At the Golconda, IL gage, the river crested at 48.62 feet on March 27. Flood stage there is 40 feet, and moderate flooding begins at 50 feet. The ferry from Hardin County, IL to Crittenden County was shut down due to high water for an extended period of time. The ferry operator reported the shutdown was longer than the Flood of 1997. Large areas of agricultural land were flooded. Crop losses were negligible since planting was not yet underway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening low pressure system moved northeast from Arkansas along a warm front that extended across southeast Missouri and southern Illinois. Surface dew points were in the upper 50's to lower 60's along and southeast of the warm front. A strong upper level trough over the western Plains produced a strong southwest flow of 60 to 80 knots at 500 mb. Winds at 850 mb were south to southwest around 40 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Countywide, a total of 69 homes were destroyed and 203 were damaged. Of the 203 damaged homes, 56 sustained major damage. Most of the damage occurred in the communities of Greenville and Powderly, where 48 homes were destroyed and 32 sustained major damage (35 to 40 percent were mobile homes). The primary focus of attention was where the fatalities occurred at a mobile home park on U.S. Highway 62 near the Highway 189 bypass. In the Muhlenberg Industrial Park, a spec building was flattened and another building was heavily damaged. A half dozen businesses and churches were damaged, including a hotel and a car dealership. The Western Kentucky Parkway was closed near Central City due to trees blocking the road. A utility pole fell on a vehicle travelling on Kentucky 601. The tornado passed within a short distance southwest of a high school, where a basketball game was in progress at the time of the storm. A large portion of the gym roof was damaged, causing heavy water damage due to rainfall entering the building. The National Guard was activated to provide security for the area. The county was declared a federal major disaster area. The average path width was 325 yards. Peak winds were estimated near 160 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were down in and around the south part of town. Street signs and power lines were down. A house was damaged and windows were blown out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked to the Ohio Valley as cold air at the surface was already entrenched. This resulted in snow and significant ice accumulations over much of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported a quarter inch of ice in Florence. The Cincinnati/Northern Kentucky airport recorded 1.6 inches of snowfall.

Many of those same areas received two to four inches of sleet and snow. Numerous trees and power lines were brought down, knocking out power to many thousands of homes. Power outages lasted up to a week. Emergency shelters were established in several counties (including Livingston, Crittenden, and Hopkins Counties) for those without power for extended periods. The National Guard was mobilized in Crittenden and Livingston Counties to help conduct door-to-door welfare checks and direct traffic. Many roads were covered by downed trees and tree limbs. Even some major roads such as U.S. Highway 60 were closed or impassable for over 24 hours in Livingston and Crittenden Counties. Travel there was banned for all but emergency traffic. Near Paducah, a fatal accident occurred shortly after the onset of freezing rain (indirect fatality). A woman lost control of her SUV in a curve and hit a tree at 45 mph. That wreck was one of 14 weather-related crashes in McCracken County. A section of the Western Kentucky Parkway was closed from the Pennyrile Parkway to Dawson Springs while tree debris was cleared from the road. All roads into and out of Dawson Springs were blocked by trees at one point.
Low pressure tracked to the Ohio Valley as cold air at the surface was already entrenched. This resulted in snow and significant ice accumulations over much of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported a quarter inch of ice in Edgewood. Another spotter in Erlanger recorded an eighth of an inch of ice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system developed over the central Gulf coast, then moved northeast across the Tennessee Valley. This surface low produced light to moderate accumulations of snow early in the morning. Following the surface low, an intensifying upper level low produced a more significant round of heavy snow during the late afternoon and nighttime hours. Four to eight inches of snow fell along and southeast of a line from Morganfield (Union County) through Princeton (Caldwell County) and Benton (Marshall County) to Murray (Calloway County). The highest amounts of 6 to 8 inches were over the Pennyrile region, including Owensboro, Henderson, Madisonville, and Hopkinsville. Isolated amounts to 10 inches were reported in Muhlenberg County. Gusty winds caused by a strong pressure gradient between the surface low and arctic high pressure over the upper Mississippi Valley caused blowing and drifting snow. Visibility was below one-quarter mile at times. Roads became snow-covered and very slippery, and a number of accidents occurred. A major wreck involved two tractor-trailer rigs on the Pennyrile Parkway in Henderson County. Three people were injured, none seriously. The northbound lanes of the parkway were shut down. About a dozen other accidents were reported in Henderson County. Drifts were commonly measured around one foot deep, with isolated reports of 2-foot drifts. Schools were closed.

A strong low pressure system moved from Texas northeast across western Kentucky. Widespread heavy rainfall along and east of the storm track brought areas of flooding. Most of western Kentucky averaged from two to four inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two people were rescued from their partially submerged vehicles on flooded Hopkinsville streets. One of the rescues occurred at a bridge over the Little River, which experienced major flooding. The bridge was completely submerged. The vehicle stalled in several feet of water. The woman climbed onto the roof of the car. Firefighters using a ladder truck extended the 100-foot ladder to her vehicle. Three firefighters guided her to safety along the ladder. In a second water rescue attempt, a car was swept downstream into a clump of trees. The driver of the car was found on the roof of the vehicle, which was in several feet of flowing water. Firefighters entered the water with life jackets and safety lines to retrieve the man. No injuries were reported in either rescue attempt.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads were flooded, and the Little River rose out of its banks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved from Texas northeast across western Kentucky. Widespread heavy rainfall along and east of the storm track brought areas of flooding. Most of western Kentucky averaged from two to four inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few roads were covered by water. West of Paducah, a pickup truck ran off a road into a flooded creek. The occupants of the truck were found standing on top of the vehicle, which was almost entirely under water. Swift water divers from the county rescue squad went out about 30 feet into the water and got the men into an inflatable boat. Both victims were treated for mild hypothermia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall amounts from 2 to 6 inches occurred over a two-day period, causing significant flooding. A very slow-moving cold front over southwest Illinois and southeast Missouri provided the focus for prolonged heavy rainfall. A deep southwest wind flow brought subtropical moisture across the frontal zone. The heaviest rainfall, from 4 to 6 inches, occurred in counties along the Ohio River. The two-day rainfall amount at the Paducah National Weather Service office was 4.40 inches. Other two-day totals included 5.72 at Henderson and 5.11 near Sturgis (Union County). The lowest totals occurred southeast of Paducah, especially in the Hopkinsville area. Two-day rainfall was 2.50 at Pembroke (Christian County) and 2.75 at Hanson (Hopkins County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads were closed due to flooding. Kentucky 109 between Clay and Wheatcroft was closed due to two feet of water across it. Most of the other roads were near Clay and in the Slaughter area. State Route 132 between Dixon and Clay was covered with water but not closed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Due to flooding of the Clarks River, several roads were underwater. A car drove into the floodwaters near Almo. The vehicle stalled and then floated about 100 yards before stopping in a ditch. The woman driver was rescued from her vehicle by a state trooper, who found her waist deep in water. She was clinging to the top of her car door with her feet dangling. The water was several feet deep. The trooper was just tall enough to wade through the water, then help her to safety. The woman was treated for hypothermia at a local hospital. Another water rescue occurred shortly afterward, when teenagers tried to cross a flooded section of highway in another area of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening low pressure system moved northeast from Arkansas along a warm front that extended across southeast Missouri and southern Illinois. Surface dew points were in the upper 50's to lower 60's along and southeast of the warm front. A strong upper level trough over the western Plains produced a strong southwest flow of 60 to 80 knots at 500 mb. Winds at 850 mb were south to southwest around 40 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several dozen homes received minor wind damage, mostly to shingles. Additional damage occurred to fewer than ten homes. Two barns were damaged. A deck was blown off a home near exit 86 on Interstate 24. Dozens of large trees and signs were blown over. The microburst was about 1.5 miles wide.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel and dime size hail was reported near exit 49 along the Pennyrile Parkway. Quarter-size hail fell at Earlington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms developed during the late evening ahead of a strong cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 67 mph gust was measured at the Fleming-Mason County Airport.  A barn roof sustained damage and utility poles were also knocked down.

A series of strong storm systems in February and March combined to raise the Ohio River above flood stage for an extended period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of the Ohio River occurred. At the Owensboro gage, the river crested at 43.3 feet on March 23. Flood stage there is 40 feet. Water spilled onto parts of Water Street in Owensboro. Low-lying bottomland fields near the river were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system in the southern plains moved east into the Ohio Valley, bringing snow that changed to sleet and freezing rain as warmer temperatures were pulled into the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A quarter inch of ice and two inches of snow was measured in Owenton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed over the southern Plains, spreading widespread heavy precipitation across western Kentucky. At the same time, high pressure over the upper Ohio Valley produced a cold easterly wind flow. The result was a crippling ice storm. Around one inch of ice caused extensive damage across parts of western Kentucky, along and north of a line from Paducah to Madisonville. Many of those same areas received two to four inches of sleet and snow. Numerous trees and power lines were brought down, knocking out power to many thousands of homes. Power outages lasted up to a week. Emergency shelters were established in several counties (including Livingston, Crittenden, and Hopkins Counties) for those without power for extended periods. The National Guard was mobilized in Crittenden and Livingston Counties to help conduct door-to-door welfare checks and direct traffic. Many roads were covered by downed trees and tree limbs. Even some major roads such as U.S. Highway 60 were closed or impassable for over 24 hours in Livingston and Crittenden Counties. Travel there was banned for all but emergency traffic. Near Paducah, a fatal accident occurred shortly after the onset of freezing rain (indirect fatality). A woman lost control of her SUV in a curve and hit a tree at 45 mph. That wreck was one of 14 weather-related crashes in McCracken County. A section of the Western Kentucky Parkway was closed from the Pennyrile Parkway to Dawson Springs while tree debris was cleared from the road. All roads into and out of Dawson Springs were blocked by trees at one point.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved northeast from eastern Kansas across the central Plains. An isolated severe storm developed in a warm south wind flow and produced large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Urban flooding occurred in Henderson, where 1 to 1.5 feet of water covered low-lying areas south of Highway 60.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved northeast from eastern Kansas across the central Plains. Strong south winds gusted to 46 mph at the Paducah National Weather Service office. An off-duty meteorologist estimated wind gusts to 50 mph blew down a tree at Kevil in Ballard County. A tree about four feet in diameter was blown over in western McCracken County.

The very stormy weather pattern of March slowly began to change by mid-month. The widespread heavy rainfall events seen in March were partially responsible for the flooding on the Green River in April. Rainfall was still above normal for April, but most of it fell early in the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Major flooding of the Green River occurred. At the Paradise gage, the river crested at 391.24 feet on the 9th. This was the fifth highest crest on record. Flood stage at Paradise is 380 feet. Many thousands of acres were under water. The acreage was primarily farm fields. Since planting season was not underway when flooding began, there was little if any crop damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The very stormy weather pattern of March slowly began to change by mid-month. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding of the Green River occurred. At the Calhoun gage, the river crested at 28.99 feet on the 10th. Flood stage at Calhoun is 23 feet. Thousands of acres were flooded in the Island, Rumsey, and Jewel City areas. The acreage was primarily farm fields. Since planting season was not underway when flooding began, there was little if any crop damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved slowly north across western Kentucky during the afternoon. Large clusters of thunderstorms produced very heavy rain. A cold front moved southeast into the Lower Ohio Valley during the night, preceded by more thunderstorms. These storms produced a report of wind damage and a tornado. Rainfall for the 24-hour period ranged from 2 to 4 inches across most of western Kentucky, with isolated amounts of 4 to 7 inches. The isolated higher amounts were mostly from Madisonville to Marion.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was over U.S. Highway 62 at the 4 mile marker, where flaggers slowed traffic. Two back roads were closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several waves of low pressure moved along a stationary front located through the Ohio Valley. These waves of low pressure brought an extended period of heavy rain to the region, with rainfall totals between three and six inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads were flooded and closed.  Banklick Creek was out of its banks in a few locations.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: State Route 36 was flooded and closed outside of Carrollton.  Several other roads were flooded throughout the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front approached the area during the afternoon hours of April 11th. Severe thunderstorms developed along and ahead of the front. The storms first moved into Eastern Kentucky shortly before 2 PM EDT. These storms affected the areas near Monticello and Somerset. A tornado was confirmed to have touched down in Wayne County. These and other storms moved northeast across the area, causing wind damage. Numerous reports of structural damage and downed trees were reported across portions of the area. Large hail was also reported with some of the storms. Very heavy rainfall with some of the storms caused flash flooding in portions of Laurel County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF2 tornado with maximum winds near 130 mph entered Wayne County from Clinton County along Pleasant Ridge Road. The tornado maintained a continuous 7 mile track east northeast before lifting 1.5 miles southwest of Monticello near Furnace Mountain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold rain fell during the daylight hours on the 7th.  A lull in the storm occurred that evening.  As temperatures began to drop slowly, freezing rain developed late at night, changing to snow before dawn on the 8th. The snow ended by the evening on the 8th.  Snow totals of 3 to 6 inches accumulated in 12 hours.  The snow fell on top of a quarter inch of ice, that had accumulated earlier from the freezing rain.

Heavy rainfall amounts from 2 to 6 inches occurred over a two-day period, causing significant flooding. A very slow-moving cold front over southwest Illinois and southeast Missouri provided the focus for prolonged heavy rainfall. A deep southwest wind flow brought subtropical moisture across the frontal zone. The heaviest rainfall, from 4 to 6 inches, occurred in counties along the Ohio River. The two-day rainfall amount at the Paducah National Weather Service office was 4.40 inches. Other two-day totals included 5.72 at Henderson and 5.11 near Sturgis (Union County). The lowest totals occurred southeast of Paducah, especially in the Hopkinsville area. Two-day rainfall was 2.50 at Pembroke (Christian County) and 2.75 at Hanson (Hopkins County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: About 20 homes in Nortonville were evacuated when floodwaters approached.

Elsewhere in the county, U.S. Highway 60 was closed at the Union County line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system developed over the central Gulf coast, then moved northeast across the Tennessee Valley. This surface low produced light to moderate accumulations of snow early in the morning. Following the surface low, an intensifying upper level low produced a more significant round of heavy snow during the late afternoon and nighttime hours. Four to eight inches of snow fell along and southeast of a line from Morganfield (Union County) through Princeton (Caldwell County) and Benton (Marshall County) to Murray (Calloway County). The highest amounts of 6 to 8 inches were over the Pennyrile region, including Owensboro, Henderson, Madisonville, and Hopkinsville. Isolated amounts to 10 inches were reported in Muhlenberg County. Gusty winds caused by a strong pressure gradient between the surface low and arctic high pressure over the upper Mississippi Valley caused blowing and drifting snow. Visibility was below one-quarter mile at times. Roads became snow-covered and very slippery, and a number of accidents occurred. A major wreck involved two tractor-trailer rigs on the Pennyrile Parkway in Henderson County. Three people were injured, none seriously. The northbound lanes of the parkway were shut down. About a dozen other accidents were reported in Henderson County. Drifts were commonly measured around one foot deep, with isolated reports of 2-foot drifts. Schools were closed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: County dispatch reported a total snow and ice accumulation of 1.5 inches, with 1 inch of ice on cars and exposed surfaces in Mount Sterling.

A low pressure system developed over the central Gulf coast, then moved northeast across the Tennessee Valley. This surface low produced light to moderate accumulations of snow early in the morning. Following the surface low, an intensifying upper level low produced a more significant round of heavy snow during the late afternoon and nighttime hours. Four to eight inches of snow fell along and southeast of a line from Morganfield (Union County) through Princeton (Caldwell County) and Benton (Marshall County) to Murray (Calloway County). The highest amounts of 6 to 8 inches were over the Pennyrile region, including Owensboro, Henderson, Madisonville, and Hopkinsville. Isolated amounts to 10 inches were reported in Muhlenberg County. Gusty winds caused by a strong pressure gradient between the surface low and arctic high pressure over the upper Mississippi Valley caused blowing and drifting snow. Visibility was below one-quarter mile at times. Roads became snow-covered and very slippery, and a number of accidents occurred. A major wreck involved two tractor-trailer rigs on the Pennyrile Parkway in Henderson County. Three people were injured, none seriously. The northbound lanes of the parkway were shut down. About a dozen other accidents were reported in Henderson County. Drifts were commonly measured around one foot deep, with isolated reports of 2-foot drifts. Schools were closed.

A strong cold front approached the region during the overnight and early morning hours of February 6th. Widespread wind damage was reported across the area, with trees and power lines blown down, and numerous reports of structural damage. Heavy rain also resulted in flash flooding across the area. Two tornadoes touched down in Eastern Kentucky as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down 2 miles north of Denniston on Highway 1240. The tornado traveled approximately 4 miles to the east northeast and lifted along Highway 1693 approximately 3 miles south of Big Woods. Two older barns were destroyed and several trees were snapped and uprooted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of strong storm systems in March raised the Mississippi River above flood stage. The record-setting heavy rainfall in southeast Missouri on the 18th and 19th was the primary cause of the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding of the Mississippi River occurred. At the New Madrid, MO gage, the river crested at 41.44 feet on March 27. Flood stage there is 34 feet, and moderate flooding begins at 40 feet. Flood gates were installed in the floodwall at Hickman. These flood gates are normally installed when the river reaches 51 feet on the Cairo gage. The ferry from Hickman, KY to Dorena, MO was shut down for over a week due to high water. Kentucky Highway 239 was closed heading toward Clinton. Large areas of agricultural land were flooded. Spring planting was not yet underway, so crop losses were negligible.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall amounts from 2 to 6 inches occurred over a two-day period, causing significant flooding. A very slow-moving cold front over southwest Illinois and southeast Missouri provided the focus for prolonged heavy rainfall. A deep southwest wind flow brought subtropical moisture across the frontal zone. The heaviest rainfall, from 4 to 6 inches, occurred in counties along the Ohio River. The two-day rainfall amount at the Paducah National Weather Service office was 4.40 inches. Other two-day totals included 5.72 at Henderson and 5.11 near Sturgis (Union County). The lowest totals occurred southeast of Paducah, especially in the Hopkinsville area. Two-day rainfall was 2.50 at Pembroke (Christian County) and 2.75 at Hanson (Hopkins County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding closed parts of U.S. Highway 62 and Kentucky 307, both major routes through the county. Highway 80 was closed west of Arlington, where a water rescue was conducted.

A developing low pressure system, and its associated warm front, moved northeast from the lower Mississippi Valley towards Eastern Kentucky. This weather system brought a mixture of snow, sleet, and freezing rain across the area on February 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The state highway garage reported that 4 inches of snow had fallen in Bethel and across the northwest portion of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front along with a strong upper level low produced a squall line that crossed central Kentucky. This set off widespread severe weather, and spawned several tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds blew shingles off some homes. Roofs on some homes had structural damage. Several trees were uprooted. Part of the structure of a gas station was blown on to Highway 627 just southwest of the Fort Boonesboro State Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of strong storm systems in February and March combined to raise the Ohio River above flood stage for an extended period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Major flooding of the Ohio River occurred. At the Grand Chain, IL gage, the river crested at 54.40 feet on March 26. Flood stage there is 42 feet, and major flooding begins at 53 feet. Floodwaters approached several homes between Oscar and Bandana, where sandbagging was done as a precaution. Large areas of agricultural land were flooded. Spring planting was not yet underway, so crop losses were negligible. Floodwaters came to the edge of U.S. Highway 51 near the approach to the Ohio River bridge. Floodwaters covered one lane of U.S. Highway 51 south of Wickliffe near a paper mill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved northeast along the eastern side of the Appalachian Mountains, spreading a mix of freezing rain, sleet, and snow across the area late of March 7th through March 8th. Several thunderstorms were also reported on the evening of March 7th, with freezing rain and sleet. The precipitation was mainly sleet and freezing rain on the evening of March 7th, but changed over to snow by the morning of March 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three inches of snow accumulated in Paintsville.  Four inches of snow was reported in Staffordsville and Flat Gap.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two inches of sleet accumulated in the southern part of the county. Six inches of sleet, with some snow mixed in, accumulated in the northern part of the county in and near Flemingsburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An occluding surface low over southwest Iowa moved northeast into southeast Minnesota. A trailing cold front pushed eastward through the Mid-Mississippi Valley. A weakening complex of storms during the morning was followed by regeneration of storms in the afternoon as a low-level jet developed.

A snowstorm developed during the early morning hours Friday March 7th. Snow and some sleet fell intermittently over the next 28 hours. Snowfall totals were highest along the Ohio River, where accumulations varied from  10 to 12 inches. Farther south...snow started later in the day and accumulations were lower.  Snow totals varied widely across the Bluegrass region, ranging from 8 inches in Frankfort to less than 4 inches south and east of Lexington. Sleet with occasional thunder fell across the eastern Bluegrass region late on the 7th...with 1 to 2 inches of sleet accumulating. Across south central Kentucky, snowfall ranged from over 8 inches north of Bowling green to just under 4 inches along the Kentucky-Tennessee border.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold northwest flow behind a departing storm system, combined with moisture off Lake Michigan, brought widespread snowfall to eastern Kentucky on February 27. Most lower elevation locations received 1 to 2 inches of snow, with localized 3 inch amounts. However, the higher terrain along the Virginia border receieved between 5 and 8 inches of snow, as upslope winds interacting with the terrain helped enhance snowfall amounts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The State Highway Garage reported that 5 to 6 inches of snow had fallen along the higher terrain on Highways 74 and 66 in the Fonde area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front approached the region during the overnight and early morning hours of February 6th. Widespread wind damage was reported across the area, with trees and power lines blown down, and numerous reports of structural damage. Heavy rain also resulted in flash flooding across the area. Two tornadoes touched down in Eastern Kentucky as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado touched down on Stepstone Road and continued east northeast for 8.7 miles. The tornado had a maximum path width of 250 yards with maximum wind speeds of around 105 mph. Two modile homes were almost completely destroyed along with several barns and outbuildings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A snowstorm developed during the early morning hours Friday March 7th. Snow and some sleet fell intermittently over the next 28 hours. Snowfall totals were highest along the Ohio River, where accumulations varied from  10 to 12 inches. Farther south...snow started later in the day and accumulations were lower.  Snow totals varied widely across the Bluegrass region, ranging from 8 inches in Frankfort to less than 4 inches south and east of Lexington. Sleet with occasional thunder fell across the eastern Bluegrass region late on the 7th...with 1 to 2 inches of sleet accumulating. Across south central Kentucky, snowfall ranged from over 8 inches north of Bowling green to just under 4 inches along the Kentucky-Tennessee border.

A warm front moved slowly north across western Kentucky during the afternoon. Large clusters of thunderstorms produced very heavy rain. A cold front moved southeast into the Lower Ohio Valley during the night, preceded by more thunderstorms. These storms produced a report of wind damage and a tornado. Rainfall for the 24-hour period ranged from 2 to 4 inches across most of western Kentucky, with isolated amounts of 4 to 7 inches. The isolated higher amounts were mostly from Madisonville to Marion.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A young girl drowned in floodwaters on Highway 855 between Frances and Salem shortly before sunrise. The child was a passenger in a vehicle driven by her mother. The vehicle plunged into about eight feet of water that was over the road at a creek crossing. The vehicle immediately began floating, and both the mother and child exited. The mother lost her grip on the child, and the child immediately went under water. A boat was used to locate the child within ten minutes. Rescue workers retrieved the child, who was caught up in debris or tree limbs in about eight feet of water. The child was given CPR and rushed to a local hospital, where she was pronounced dead. The area is known to flood by local residents, and it was marked by an orange high water sign. The mother was new to the area and likely unfamiliar with the flooding problems there. About 5.5 inches of rain had fallen the night before the drowning. Countywide, numerous roads were covered with water. Several roads were closed for a few days, including Highway 120.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An occluding surface low over southwest Iowa moved northeast into southeast Minnesota. A trailing cold front pushed eastward through the Mid-Mississippi Valley. A weakening complex of storms during the morning was followed by regeneration of storms in the afternoon as a low-level jet developed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four roads were closed on the west side of town.

Several rounds of showers and thunderstorms moved east along a stationary front near the Ohio River on June 4th. Some of these storms moved into northern Fleming County in Kentucky. These storms produced damaging winds, resulting in downed trees and tree limbs. Hail the size of quarters was also reported. In addition to the large hail and damaging winds, flash flooding also occurred. The ground was already saturated from the previous day's rainfall, with another 1 to 2 inches falling on the 3rd. Several roads had at least a half a foot of water flowing over them for a time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One large tree limb was blown down at the court house by thunderstorm wind gusts.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Marion, some families were evacuated due to high water. The rescue squad assisted a mother and two children on Highway 1077 in Shady Grove. About a foot of water was over U.S. Highway 60 about three miles west of Marion. U.S. 60 was under several feet of water east of Marion, where a tractor-trailer became stranded. Several feet of water flooded U.S. Highway 641. Highway 91 was flooded and covered with large amounts of mud and rock.

A cold front along with a strong upper level low produced a squall line that crossed central Kentucky. This set off widespread severe weather, and spawned several tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was the end point of a tornado that crossed into Monroe County, Kentucky from Sumner County, Tennessee. The tornado moved through a rural area of Cumberland County and lifted about three miles southwest of Marrowbone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms developed during the evening in association with a 500 mb shortwave trough moving east-southeast across Illinois. At the surface, a weak trough of low pressure extended southwest across the mid Mississippi Valley. Most of the severe weather reports were from a cluster of storms that tracked southeast from Illinois across the Marion and Princeton areas to around Hopkinsville.

Across south central Kentucky, snowfall ranged from over 8 inches north of Bowling green to just under 4 inches along the Kentucky-Tennessee border.
A fast moving cold front coupled with an upper level low brought some heavy rains and a few severe thunderstorms to parts of north central and east central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds damaged roofs along Interstate 64 in the western part of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms developed during the evening in association with a 500 mb shortwave trough moving east-southeast across Illinois. At the surface, a weak trough of low pressure extended southwest across the mid Mississippi Valley. Most of the severe weather reports were from a cluster of storms that tracked southeast from Illinois across the Marion and Princeton areas to around Hopkinsville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down, along with power lines. Two roofs were damaged. A few roadways were partially blocked.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to 2 inches of sleet fell accompanied by thunder Friday evening the 7th of March.

A deepening low pressure system moved northeast from Arkansas along a warm front that extended across southeast Missouri and southern Illinois. Surface dew points were in the upper 50's to lower 60's along and southeast of the warm front. A strong upper level trough over the western Plains produced a strong southwest flow of 60 to 80 knots at 500 mb. Winds at 850 mb were south to southwest around 40 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were uprooted and snapped during this very brief tornado. Peak winds were estimated from 85 to 90 mph.

A stationary frontal boundary over the lower Ohio Valley brought some training thunderstorms to parts of east central Kentucky. These thunderstorms over previously saturated ground caused some river flooding over the Licking River basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Licking River at Blue Licks Spring crested at 25.2 feet around 815 PM EST on March 28. Flood stage at Blue Licks Spring is 25 feet. Minor flooding of bottom land occurs at this level.

Severe thunderstorms moved into south central Kentucky from Tennessee, including a super cell thunderstorm that produced four separate tornadoes that damaged numerous homes in Clinton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado touched down west of Highway 639 south of Wago, moving east-northeast for approximately .4 mile before lifting.  The tornado  destroyed a barn and heavily damaged a grain silo along Highway 639, and downed several large trees. Trees were felled on a farmhouse and a late model SUV just north of the silo, destroying the SUV and doing extensive damage to the home.  Other minor damage was sustained east of Highway 639 to several outbuildings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system tracked east-northeast across central Illinois during the morning. Strong west winds developed in the wake of a strong cold front that raced eastward across the Lower Ohio and Mid Mississippi Valleys. Peak wind gusts were generally from 45 to 50 mph at most locations. Numerous tree limbs were blown down across western Kentucky. In Hopkins County, a tree was blown down across the southbound lane of Highway 1337. Power outages in Marshall and Calloway Counties were caused by trees blown onto power lines. In Trigg County, a large tree limb fell on a car in Cadiz. The car was severely damaged. Three small trees fell across city streets in Cadiz, closing them.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple miles southeast of Farmersville, a barn was levelled. Nearby barns were damaged, along with roofs of houses. Trees were blown down, mainly across northern parts of the county. Several roads were blocked, including Highways 139 and 70 near Creswell. Power outages occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moving across the area brought severe thunderstorms with damaging winds and large hail to portions of Eastern Kentucky during the morning hours of May 11th. Numerous reports of downed trees and power lines were received. Hail up to the size of golf balls was also reported. Two tornadoes touched down as well, with one in Leslie County, and the other in Whitley County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thundestorm wind gusts downed several power lines in the Browning Acres neighborhood.

A frontal system and upper level low brought widespread heavy rains and flooding to central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three mobile homes had water in them and the families had to be evacuated. Numerous roads around the county were closed due to high water, and some roads were washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system and upper level low brought widespread heavy rains and flooding to central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Stoner Creek at Paris crested at 23.5 feet around 1005 PM EST on April 4. Flood stage at Paris is 18 feet. Moderate flooding occurs at this level, with water entering some homes below the water plant. Parts of the city park are flooded. The hardest hit areas were Sandy Bottoms, Houston Avenue, and the Garrard Park area, where water entered the lower levels of some homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms intensified and increased in coverage late in the afternoon and evening as they developed southward across western Kentucky and southeast Missouri. The storms quickly intensified in the hot, humid, and unstable atmosphere. As a cold front approached from the northwest, some storms maintained severe levels well into the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous large trees were down, mainly in the central part of the county near Briensburg. A concentration of downed trees was reported in areas along Highway 58, especially from U.S. Highway 641 to U.S. Highway 68 in the Briensburg area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few large limbs were blown down.

A cold front with some upper level support set off isolated severe thunderstorms over parts of north and east central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed trees and power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front with some upper level support set off isolated severe thunderstorms over parts of north and east central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds damaged the fire station in Bagdad. Trees and power lines were downed between Bagdad and Cropper.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front with some upper level support set off isolated severe thunderstorms over parts of north and east central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front with some upper level support set off isolated severe thunderstorms over parts of north and east central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed trees over much of the southern part of Jefferson County. Some of the trees were blown on to cars or blocked roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved into south central Kentucky from Tennessee, including a super cell thunderstorm that produced four separate tornadoes that damaged numerous homes in Clinton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The second touchdown of a tornado in with a storm that moved across Clinton County produced EF-1 damage in a path just under 1/2 mile.  Four utility poles were snapped along U.S. Highway 127, as were several large diameter trees in a narrow path on a wooded hill to the east of the highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved into south central Kentucky from Tennessee, including a super cell thunderstorm that produced four separate tornadoes that damaged numerous homes in Clinton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The fourth touchdown in this family of tornadoes produced by a cyclic supercell did the most damage in Clinton County as it hit several residences in a rural subdivision along Pleasure Ridge Road.  After moving through uninhabited hillside for a mile, uprooting and snapping trees in a nearly quarter-mile wide path, the tornado tore the roof of a ranch-style brick home and destroyed its nearby outbuildings.  Continuing along parallel to Pleasure Ridge Road, the tornado destroyed a mobile home, barn, and grain  silo in its path, and heavily damaged at least three other homes before exiting the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Squall lines with embedded bow echoes formed near a cold front.  The front was associated with a deep surface low pressure in the Midwest and a strong upper level trough.
Squall lines with embedded bow echoes formed near a cold front.  The front was associated with a deep surface low pressure in the Midwest and a strong upper level trough.
Squall lines with embedded bow echoes formed near a cold front.  The front was associated with a deep surface low pressure in the Midwest and a strong upper level trough.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severel trees were blown down in the Blaine and Lowmansville area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Squall lines with embedded bow echoes formed near a cold front.  The front was associated with a deep surface low pressure in the Midwest and a strong upper level trough.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado briefly touched down in the Goose Creek and Grahn Fork vicinity, east of Olive Hill.  Winds were estimated near 100 mph.  Along the path, numerous trees were snapped off.  Many of these trees were evergreen.  A roof was blown off a manufactured home along Goose Creek.  About a half dozen other homes had roof damage.  Outbuildings and a car port were also damaged. Some telephone poles were snapped off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved slowly south, triggering some thunderstorms with heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter measured storm-total rainfall of 2.40 inches of rain at the Highway 231 bypass. Flooding of the typical flood-prone areas occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An occluding surface low over southwest Iowa moved northeast into southeast Minnesota. A trailing cold front pushed eastward through the Mid-Mississippi Valley. A weakening complex of storms during the morning was followed by regeneration of storms in the afternoon as a low-level jet developed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Standing water was observed along Highway 365 between mile markers 1 and 2 and over Highway 664 at the 6-mile marker.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An occluding surface low over southwest Iowa moved northeast into southeast Minnesota. A trailing cold front pushed eastward through the Mid-Mississippi Valley. A weakening complex of storms during the morning was followed by regeneration of storms in the afternoon as a low-level jet developed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of water was flowing over U.S. Highway 41 at Earlington. Both sides of the road were flooded over on U.S. Highway 41 by the county central high school. A spotter reported two to three inches of rain in an hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved slowly north across western Kentucky during the afternoon. Large clusters of thunderstorms produced very heavy rain. A cold front moved southeast into the Lower Ohio Valley during the night, preceded by more thunderstorms. These storms produced a report of wind damage and a tornado. Rainfall for the 24-hour period ranged from 2 to 4 inches across most of western Kentucky, with isolated amounts of 4 to 7 inches. The isolated higher amounts were mostly from Madisonville to Marion.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A vehicle was swept off a secondary road in the southeastern part of the county. The fire and rescue team used a boat to rescue the driver. Ten state roads were closed by high water, including Kentucky Highway 56 at the 9 mile marker.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved slowly north across western Kentucky during the afternoon. Large clusters of thunderstorms produced very heavy rain. A cold front moved southeast into the Lower Ohio Valley during the night, preceded by more thunderstorms. These storms produced a report of wind damage and a tornado. Rainfall for the 24-hour period ranged from 2 to 4 inches across most of western Kentucky, with isolated amounts of 4 to 7 inches. The isolated higher amounts were mostly from Madisonville to Marion.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Major flash flooding occurred in Madisonville. Seven families were evacuated from homes on one street. Fourteen people were evacuated by boat from a mobile home park. A Red Cross shelter was opened for evacuees. One car was stranded on Main Street in Madisonville, where between one and two feet of water was over portions of the street. A one-mile section of U.S. Highway 41 was closed. The parking lot of Parkway Plaza Mall in Madisonville was underwater. Standing water was over sections of a street near the mall. Ditches along Highway 112 were overflowing near Earlington. Flooding was reported at the Madisonville Country Club.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved slowly north across western Kentucky during the afternoon. Large clusters of thunderstorms produced very heavy rain. A cold front moved southeast into the Lower Ohio Valley during the night, preceded by more thunderstorms. These storms produced a report of wind damage and a tornado. Rainfall for the 24-hour period ranged from 2 to 4 inches across most of western Kentucky, with isolated amounts of 4 to 7 inches. The isolated higher amounts were mostly from Madisonville to Marion.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near Mortons Gap, the entrance to the southbound Pennyrile Parkway on-ramp was under two to three feet of water. Highway 813 at Mortons Gap was under an inch of water. Countywide, many roads were covered with water. Roads were closed, especially in the north portion of the county. Highway 109 was closed for a few days. Main Street in Madisonville was partially closed. Flooding caused significant damage to at least three businesses in a mall in Madisonville. Up to six inches of water on the floor of a grocery store damaged inventory. Additional damage to inventory occurred when power was cut to the mall for safety reasons. The mall parking lot was flooded up to a depth of three feet, and a car was submerged over the headlights.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The very stormy weather pattern of March slowly began to change by mid-month. The widespread record-setting rainfall events seen in March were responsible for much of the flooding on the Ohio River in April. Rainfall was still above normal for April, but excessive rainfall was confined to a few locations where storms trained.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of the Ohio River occurred. The river remained above flood stage from early March through late April. On the Grand Chain, Illinois gage, the crest in April was 53.20 feet on the 12th. Flood stage at Grand Chain is 42 feet. Many low-lying areas were flooded, primarily farm fields. Crop damage was still negligible, since the flooding started before planting season.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A developing low pressure system over the Texas Gulf Coast moved northeast towards Eastern Kentucky. This brought a mix of sleet and freezing rain, with a little snow as well, to Eastern Kentucky on February 21st into the 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three tenths of an inch of ice accretion from freezing rain occurred in Flemingsburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving across Kentucky during the afternoon hours of March 4th caused the development of severe thunderstorms across the area. Numerous reports of downed trees and power lines were received. Several mobile homes were blown over and destroyed as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trailers were blown over by thunderstorm wind gusts. A trailer in the Science Hill Area on highway 635 was destroyed.  Trees and power lines were also blown down both the southern and southwestern portions of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved slowly north across western Kentucky during the afternoon. Large clusters of thunderstorms produced very heavy rain. A cold front moved southeast into the Lower Ohio Valley during the night, preceded by more thunderstorms. These storms produced a report of wind damage and a tornado. Near Bell City, about 30 percent of the roof on a large farm equipment building was blown off. Some siding was removed from another. The damage path was broken. The peak wind was estimated at 90 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The very stormy weather pattern of March slowly began to change by mid-month. The widespread record-setting rainfall events seen in March were responsible for much of the flooding on the Mississippi River in April. Rainfall was still above normal for April, but excessive rainfall was confined to a few locations where storms trained.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding of the Mississippi River occurred. At the New Madrid, Missouri gage, the river remained above flood stage from mid March through late April. The crest in April was 40.72 feet on the 16th. Flood stage at New Madrid is 34 feet. Ferry service from Hickman, KY to Dorena, MO was suspended due to the high water. Kentucky 1129 and a section of Kentucky 94 was closed. Many low-lying areas were flooded, primarily farm fields. Since planting season was not underway when flooding began, there was little if any crop damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved northeast from eastern Kansas across the central Plains. Strong south winds gusted to 46 mph at the Paducah National Weather Service office. An off-duty meteorologist estimated wind gusts to 50 mph blew down a tree at Kevil in Ballard County. A tree about four feet in diameter was blown over in western McCracken County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved slowly north across western Kentucky during the afternoon. Large clusters of thunderstorms produced very heavy rain. A cold front moved southeast into the Lower Ohio Valley during the night, preceded by more thunderstorms. These storms produced a report of wind damage and a tornado. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven state highways were covered by water, including U.S. Highway 641 near the Lyon County line. State Route 139 was covered with water north of Princeton near the 14 mile marker. A bridge was washed out on a secondary road near Creswell. Other less travelled roads across the county were covered.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall amounts from 2 to 6 inches occurred over a two-day period, causing significant flooding. A very slow-moving cold front over southwest Illinois and southeast Missouri provided the focus for prolonged heavy rainfall. A deep southwest wind flow brought subtropical moisture across the frontal zone. The heaviest rainfall, from 4 to 6 inches, occurred in counties along the Ohio River. The two-day rainfall amount at the Paducah National Weather Service office was 4.40 inches. Other two-day totals included 5.72 at Henderson and 5.11 near Sturgis (Union County). The lowest totals occurred southeast of Paducah, especially in the Hopkinsville area. Two-day rainfall was 2.50 at Pembroke (Christian County) and 2.75 at Hanson (Hopkins County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Major urban flooding occurred in Owensboro, where the city used all 65 of its high water signs. Water was up to three feet deep in some spots. Wakes caused by vehicles driving through floodwaters aggravated damage to businesses and residential property. County schools were closed. The Red Cross opened a shelter for any evacuees. Due to flooding around gas meters, utilities were turned off to homes on a couple of streets. A landslide occurred along U.S. Highway 60 near Stanley. The road was closed for repairs. Fifteen to 20 roads were under water in the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening hours of May 9th, thunderstorms developed north of a stationary front situated over Tennessee. A couple of reports of large hail were received across Eastern Kentucky.

Storms intensified and increased in coverage late in the afternoon and evening as they developed southward across western Kentucky and southeast Missouri. The storms quickly intensified in the hot, humid, and unstable atmosphere. As a cold front approached from the northwest, some storms maintained severe levels well into the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were down, including two that blocked roads. State Routes 70 and 453 were each blocked.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms intensified and increased in coverage late in the afternoon and evening as they developed southward across western Kentucky and southeast Missouri. The storms quickly intensified in the hot, humid, and unstable atmosphere. As a cold front approached from the northwest, some storms maintained severe levels well into the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was over several streets. At least one street was impassable to small vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system and upper level low brought widespread heavy rains and flooding to central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sections of Kentucky Highways 100 and 678 were closed due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system and upper level low brought widespread heavy rains and flooding to central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Because of high water, the Kentucky Highway Department closed several roads near the Green River Parkway corridor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system and upper level low brought widespread heavy rains and flooding to central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Licking River at Blue Licks Spring crested at 31.4 feet around 1045 PM EST on April 4. Flood stage at the site is 25 feet. Minor flooding occurs at this level, with some flooding of cultivated crop land.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system and upper level low brought widespread heavy rains and flooding to central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The South Fork Licking River at Cynthiana crested at 20.1 feet around 530 AM EST on April 5. Flood stage at Cynthiana is 20 feet. Minor flooding occurs at this level. A large section of the city park floods, and water backs up into some streets on the west side of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system and upper level low brought widespread heavy rains and flooding to central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rolling Fork River at Boston crested at 43.6 feet around 930 AM EST on April 6. Flood stage at Boston is 35 feet. Moderate flooding occurs at this level, with a few homes and trailers flooded outside of Boston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system and upper level low brought widespread heavy rains and flooding to central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Barren River at Bowling Green crested at 29.4 feet around 615 AM CST on April 5. Flood stage at Bowling Green is 28 feet. Minor flooding occurs at this level, with cultivated land flooding about eight miles downstream from the gauge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system and upper level low brought widespread heavy rains and flooding to central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Green River at Munfordville crested at 30.5 feet around 1245 PM CST on April 5. Flood stage at the site is 28 feet. Minor flooding occurs at this level, with the city park flooded under the U.S. Highway 31W bridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system and upper level low brought widespread heavy rains and flooding to central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Green River at Brownsville crested at 22.1 feet around 245 AM CST on April 6. Flood stage at Brownsville is 18 feet. Minor flooding occurs at this level, with the river overflowing the lock walls and some lower banks and creeks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system and upper level low brought widespread heavy rains and flooding to central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kentucky Highway 403 between mile markers 4 and 5 was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms produced damaging winds and small hail  across South Central Kentucky.
A line of severe thunderstorms produced damaging winds and small hail  across South Central Kentucky.

A slow moving cold front approached the area during the afternoon hours of April 11th. Severe thunderstorms developed along and ahead of the front. The storms first moved into Eastern Kentucky shortly before 2 PM EDT. These storms affected the areas near Monticello and Somerset. A tornado was confirmed to have touched down in Wayne County. These and other storms moved northeast across the area, causing wind damage. Numerous reports of structural damage and downed trees were reported across portions of the area. Large hail was also reported with some of the storms. Very heavy rainfall with some of the storms caused flash flooding in portions of Laurel County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pittsburgh School Road was closed due to flood waters. Four to 5 inches of water were running across Slate Lick Road.  Several homes were evacuated due to flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved into south central Kentucky from Tennessee, including a super cell thunderstorm that produced four separate tornadoes that damaged numerous homes in Clinton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State and county roads in the southern part of the county, including Kentucky Highway 100, were closed due to flowing water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system and upper level low brought widespread heavy rains and flooding to central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Green River at Woodbury crested at 40.7 feet around 4 AM CST on April 7. Flood stage at Woodbury is 33 feet. Minor flooding occurs at this level, with low spots on North and South Church Streets in Woodbury flooded, and several county roads covered with water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system and upper level low brought widespread heavy rains and flooding to central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Green River at Rochester crested at 22.8 feet around 5 AM CST on April 8. Flood stage at Rochester is 17 feet. Minor flooding occurs at this level, with ferry operations suspended.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system and upper level low brought widespread heavy rains and flooding to central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rough River at Dundee crested at 28.9 feet around 330 AM EST on April 5. Flood stage at Dundee is 25 feet. Moderate flooding occurs at this level, with the post office flooded and sections of Kentucky Highway 69 covered with water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moving across the area brought severe thunderstorms with damaging winds and large hail to portions of Eastern Kentucky during the morning hours of May 11th. Numerous reports of downed trees and power lines were received. Hail up to the size of golf balls was also reported. Two tornadoes touched down as well, with one in Leslie County, and the other in Whitley County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado with wind speeds of around 95 mph briefly touched down one mile southeast of Wooton. Large trees were either snapped off or blown over. One tree fell onto a power line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved into south central Kentucky from Tennessee, including a super cell thunderstorm that produced four separate tornadoes that damaged numerous homes in Clinton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system and upper level low brought widespread heavy rains and flooding to central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several vehicles were submerged in the Louisville area, but no injuries or water rescues were reported. Numerous roads were closed due to flooding around the Louisville Metro area. Some of the closures included: a lane of Interstate 65 at the Woodbine exit, Third Street at Eastern Parkway, Breckinridge Lane at Six Mile Lane, Outer Loop at Preston Highway, and Outer Loop at New Cut Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moving across the area brought severe thunderstorms with damaging winds and large hail to portions of Eastern Kentucky during the morning hours of May 11th. Numerous reports of downed trees and power lines were received. Hail up to the size of golf balls was also reported. Two tornadoes touched down as well, with one in Leslie County, and the other in Whitley County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado touched down 1 mile west of the Cumberland Falls State Park on Highway 90 and continued into Whitley County and across the park. Wind speeds with this tornado were estimated at 105 mph. Significant damage was done to the park supervisor's residence. Extensive numbers of large trees were blown across roads which made access to parts of the park impossible until they were cleared away. Numerous trees were snapped or uprooted with some of the trees falling on cars and powerlines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moving across the area brought severe thunderstorms with damaging winds and large hail to portions of Eastern Kentucky during the morning hours of May 11th. Two tornadoes touched down as well, with one in Leslie County, and the other in Whitley County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 59 mph wind gust was measured by the Triangle Mountain RAWS observation platform.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved into south central Kentucky from Tennessee, including a super cell thunderstorm that produced four separate tornadoes that damaged numerous homes in Clinton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As the storm continued eastward, several homes and outbuildings were heavily damaged by the third tornado touchdown produced by the supercell.  In addition to the structural damage covering nearly 1 1/2 miles, scores of trees were downed as were utility lines.  A witness at the end of this tornado's path reported seeing two tornadoes on the ground simultaneously, as the fourth tornado produced by this supercell touched down about 1/4 mile north of the ending point of the third twister.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increasing moisture quickly moved up the Ohio River Valley on the morning of the 3rd into a developing east to west frontal zone.  The first of several thunderstorm complexes moved from west to east across northeast Kentucky around midday on the 3rd.  The west to east band of training thunderstorms shifted slightly north overnight.  As a result, a lull in the rain was seen over northeast Kentucky.  However, the convection sank back south during the daylight hours on the 4th. More thunderstorms crossed northeast Kentucky.   ||Rain totals over about a 30 hour period were heaviest toward the Ohio River, compared to points just to the south.  Warnock in Greenup County had 4.02 inches, while Ashland of Boyd County measured 4.29 inches. Meanwhile, Grayson measured 2.76 inches.||The ground was initially drier than counties further north in Ohio and West Virginia, but the final rounds of thunderstorms on the 4th did produce some flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Outbuildings and trees were blown down.

Isolated wind damage occurred with storms along and ahead of a weak surface cold front that moved southeast from Missouri to the Lower Ohio Valley. The storms formed during the heat and humidity of the afternoon, organizing into a bow-shaped structure that produced strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated up to 60 mph in Grahamville. Some debris was on roads in the west part of the county, including small tree limbs. A couple of trees were down across roads in Paducah and Lone Oak. An off-duty meteorologist estimated winds to around 60 mph in Paducah.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms formed during the heat of the afternoon, aided by a weak surface trough that extended from northern Arkansas northeast across the Lower Ohio Valley. Tropical moisture combined with the slow movement of the storms resulted in locally very heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 60 mph was measured in the southern part of Madisonville. Large tree limbs were down, blocking a few roads and damaging a fence. A small porch on a restaurant was damaged, along with a gazebo.

The freezing level was only 11,000 to 12,000 feet.||The severe weather reports were large hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increasing moisture quickly moved up the Ohio River Valley on the morning of the 3rd into a developing east to west frontal zone.  The first of several thunderstorm complexes moved from west to east across northeast Kentucky around midday on the 3rd.  The west to east band of training thunderstorms shifted slightly north overnight.  As a result, a lull in the rain was seen over northeast Kentucky.  However, the convection sank back south during the daylight hours on the 4th. More thunderstorms crossed northeast Kentucky.   ||Rain totals over about a 30 hour period were heaviest toward the Ohio River, compared to points just to the south.  Warnock in Greenup County had 4.02 inches, while Ashland of Boyd County measured 4.29 inches. Meanwhile, Grayson measured 2.76 inches.||The ground was initially drier than counties further north in Ohio and West Virginia, but the final rounds of thunderstorms on the 4th did produce some flooding.
